questions.question: What should your candidates complete before beginning their in-person training?

options.a: All of the Divemaster digital learning
options.b: Sections 1-3 of the Divemaster digital learning
options.c: The Diver Stress and Rescue and Open Water Diver digital learning
options.d: The Divemaster digital learning and the candidate water fitness evaluation

questions.explanation: Front-load all academic content via digital learning so in-person time is reserved for application + skills — that's where Pro time has the highest value. (b) Sections 1-3 is partial — they'd hit content gaps during practical sessions. (c) prereq-program materials aren't DM curriculum. (d) water fitness eval is itself part of in-person, not pre-work.

questions.question: What additional training materials are required for the Divemaster program?

options.c: Dive Briefing and Dive Guiding Evaluation forms

questions.explanation: DM core competencies = briefing certified divers and guiding them on dives (mssi-inst-359), so the program-specific evaluation forms cover exactly those skills. (a), (b) and (d) are teaching-eval instruments belonging to AI / Instructor levels — DM has no teaching authority, so teaching-eval forms aren't part of DM materials.

questions.question: Candidates gaining the Dive Guide certification may:

options.a: Guide friends and family on recreational dives
options.c: Work for a training center, dive resort, or dive boat

questions.explanation: Dive Guide scope is NON-COMMERCIAL recreational guiding (mssi-inst-359) — friends/family on local dives fits cleanly. (c) commercial work at a TC/resort/boat typically requires the Divemaster rating (which adds the commercial-guiding authority and the additional Pro responsibilities). (d) DG can be a stepping stone toward DM, but registration also depends on other prereqs (40 logged dives, 18+, S&R cert) — not automatic. (b) all-correct sweeps in the commercial-scope item.

questions.question: Candidates can earn the Divemaster rating by completing their training and:

options.b: Teaching at least five Scuba Skills Updates
options.c: Meeting the requirements for maintaining active status
options.d: Registering for an Instructor Training Course

questions.explanation: Two parts to earning ACTIVE DM rating: complete the training + meet active-status requirements (annual renewal fee, online renewal, TC affiliation, insurance where legally required — mssi-inst-107, 170, 172). Without active status the rating sits inactive and unusable. (a) Decompression Diving is Tec, not DM territory. (b) teaching SSUs requires DM authority already — chicken-and-egg. (d) ITC registration is the NEXT Pro-path step, not DM completion criterion.

questions.question: After completing their training, Divemasters must ____ before earning their certification card.

options.a: Complete the requirements for maintaining active status
options.d: Complete the Science of Diving program

questions.explanation: Science of Diving (a Diving Knowledge specialty — mssi-inst-299) is a SSI corequisite for the DM cert card — it provides the theoretical depth (physics, physiology, equipment, environment) that pure DM training doesn't fully cover, and that DM-level Pros need for downstream teaching prep. (a) active-status maintenance is the rating-activation requirement (mssi-inst-364), not the cert-card-issuance gate. (c) registering happens at the start of training. (b) all-correct sweeps in irrelevant items.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Divemaster Practical Application Sessions?

options.b: Prepare a candidate to teach the Perfect Buoyancy program
options.c: Evaluate a candidate's abilities to complete the duties of a Divemaster
options.d: Introduce a candidate to the duties of a professional

questions.explanation: DM Practical Application Sessions = the evaluation phase: candidate has to PERFORM the DM duties (dive briefing, dive guiding, in-water scenario response, surface management) under assessment. (b) Perfect Buoyancy teaching is OWI authority, not DM. (d) introduction to Pro duties happens earlier in training; Application Sessions are the proof-of-competence stage, not intro. (a) all-correct sweeps in the wrong items.

questions.question: A candidate must demonstrate at least a _____ level of proficiency during the Open Water Diver Skills Workshop.
questions.explanation: DM candidate doing the OWD Skills Workshop must perform at EXPERT level — clean, slow, exaggerated, demonstration-quality. Consistent with mssi-inst-349 (Pro skill must be ABOVE student level). DMs are role models: students copy whatever they see in the water, so anything less than expert quality propagates errors. (a) recreational and (b) entry are student-target levels, too low. (d) 'professional' is vague and not the SSI verbatim.

questions.question: The Dive Guiding Evaluation:

options.a: Can be combined with the Dive Briefing Evaluation
options.c: Should only be conducted in the pool/confined water
options.d: Should be one hour long to increase training value

questions.explanation: Briefing + Guiding evals are a natural pair (briefing leads into guiding the same dive) — combining them is efficient and gives the evaluator a continuous view of the candidate's flow. (b) 'no problems' would defeat the purpose — evaluations need realistic scenarios. (c) pool-only contradicts the role: guiding mostly happens in OW. (d) one-hour is an arbitrary fixed length not in the standard.

questions.question: The Candidate Water Fitness Evaluation:

options.a: Must be completed after the Dive Guiding evaluation
options.b: Is optional, if the candidate has already completed the Student Water Fitness Evaluation
options.c: Is identical to the entry-level Student Water Fitness Evaluation
options.d: Evaluates a candidate's professional-level in-water comfort

questions.explanation: Pro-level fitness eval is tougher than the student version — DM candidates need extra reserves to manage rescues, towing, surface work over longer durations (swim distance, tread-water time, gear-tow, breath-hold). (a) ordering relative to Dive Guiding eval isn't fixed by standard. (b) is wrong — Pro fitness is separately required, not waivable by prior student-level test. (c) inverts difficulty — Pro level is harder by design.

questions.question: Divemaster candidates must successfully complete at least ____ Dive Briefing Evaluation, but should complete more if possible.
questions.explanation: Standard minimum = 1 successful Dive Briefing Evaluation. The 'should complete more if possible' wording flags it as a floor, not a target — more reps build the candidate's confidence and refine the briefing structure. (b), (c), (d) over-specify the minimum.

questions.question: What kind of scenarios should be included in the Dive Guiding Evaluation?
questions.explanation: DG Evaluation tests core competence: realistic enough to look like a real dive, but with SIMPLE problems so the evaluator sees the candidate's basic decision-making without overload. (a) and (b) over-stress with complex scenarios — that's not the entry-eval bar. (d) no problems at all leaves nothing to evaluate.

questions.question: The Diver Stress & Rescue Skills Workshop should be done:

options.b: Only if time and scheduling permits

questions.explanation: Workshop scenarios should mirror real rescue conditions — close enough that competence transfers to a real incident, but not contrived. (b) frames the Workshop as optional; it's a required part of DM training, not a bonus. (c) over-restricts environment; pool / confined work fits some scenarios. (d) intentionally cranking up stress beyond realistic crosses into hazing — defeats the learning purpose.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Deep Dive Guiding Evaluation?

options.a: To prepare future Divemasters to guide certified divers on deep dives
options.b: Divemasters can qualify as Night & Limited Visibility or Deep Diving Specialty Instructors
options.c: Deep dives will be the primary responsibility of Divemasters worldwide
options.d: To increase the difficulty of the Divemaster program

questions.explanation: DMs frequently guide certified divers at the deeper end of recreational range (OWD 18 m, AOW 30 m), so the eval verifies the candidate can manage a group safely at depth — gas-monitoring discipline, narcosis awareness, ascent control. (b) Specialty Instructor ratings are a separate teaching pathway, not a DM-eval outcome. (c) over-claims — deep diving is one of many DM duties, not the primary one. (d) difficulty-for-its-own-sake is a non-rationale.

questions.question: Which of the following is a benefit of dive industry media?

options.a: Shares your experiences with friends and family
options.b: Attracts new customers to the sport
options.d: Informs divers and dive professionals about news, events, and technologies

questions.explanation: Three legitimate roles of dive industry media: (a) sharing experiences (social posts, blogs, photo galleries) reinforces the sport's culture and word-of-mouth; (b) acquisition — well-produced media reaches non-divers and pulls them in; (d) industry information — news, gear releases, conservation events keep Pros and active divers current. All three reinforce each other.

questions.question: Equipment manufacturers benefit the dive industry by:

options.d: Researching and creating new medical procedures

questions.explanation: Manufacturers' core industry contribution = R&D and innovation: lighter regulators, drysuit valve safety, dive computer decompression algorithms, BCD ergonomics. (c) destination promotion is the travel industry's lane (operators, tourism boards). (d) medical research lives with DAN, hyperbaric medicine groups, dive physiology academia — not equipment makers. (b) all-correct sweeps in out-of-scope items.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true?

options.a: The actions of a professional have little to no effect on a diver's career
options.b: Capable and confident divers are more likely to continue to dive
options.c: Divers with a poor training experience will continue to visit your training center

questions.explanation: Causality chain that drives the whole SSI ecosystem: quality training → competence + confidence → diver keeps diving → lifetime value for the diver and the industry. (a) inverts the truth — a Pro's behaviour is a major variable in whether a student becomes a lifelong diver (mssi-inst-115). (c) inverts the retention reality — poor experience = no return. (d) all-correct sweeps in the false claims.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best resource for a diver looking for additional training and experiences?
questions.explanation: TC = the whole ecosystem under one roof: certified instructors, equipment access, programs, social network, travel options, gear servicing. (a) unaffiliated Pros lack QMS oversight + standards backing — risk + no quality assurance. (b) non-diving people can't advise on training. (d) internet forums have wildly inconsistent quality and often pass on outdated or wrong information.

questions.question: What is the sequence of the Customer Loyalty Cycle?
questions.explanation: Logical funnel: (1) ACQUISITION — Try Scuba / intro programs pull non-divers in; (2) DEVELOPMENT — entry-level cert builds the foundational skills; (3) COMMITMENT — gear ownership, con-ed, emotional buy-in deepens engagement; (4) RETENTION — long-term relationship, repeat business, advocacy / referrals. Each phase feeds the next; the order is causal. The other options scramble the sequence and break the funnel logic.

questions.question: Travel agencies benefit the dive industry by:

options.b: Promoting dive industry news and new techniques
options.d: Connecting divers and training centers to new and exciting dive destinations

questions.explanation: Travel agencies bridge divers and TCs to destinations — booking trips to Egypt, Truk, Galapagos, Mediterranean liveaboards, group expeditions, package deals. (a) R&D is the manufacturers' lane (mssi-inst-375). (b) news / techniques is dive industry media (mssi-inst-374). (c) creating + certifying divers is the TC + Pro role, not travel agencies.

questions.question: Why do you need to earn your customer's trust and respect?

options.a: Because it is the only way to get them to spend money
options.b: Because they can always choose to take their business elsewhere
options.c: Because they will buy less equipment from you
options.d: Because it's required by the SSI Training Center Agreement

questions.explanation: Trust + respect = the foundation of retention. Divers have free choice across TCs and Pros — without trust they walk to a competitor at the next opportunity. (a) frames trust as a sales lever only — too transactional, not the whole picture. (c) inverts cause and effect. (d) trust isn't a contractual obligation under the TC Agreement; it's a relationship outcome.

questions.question: By completing the Acquisition phase, your customers:

options.a: Choose not to do business with you
options.c: Decide to do business with you
options.d: Choose to become a lifelong customer

questions.explanation: Acquisition = first stage of the Loyalty Cycle (mssi-inst-378). Output: the customer has decided to engage — signed up for Try Scuba, paid the OWD deposit, booked the first session. That's the WIN of acquisition. (a) is acquisition failing. (b) Pro path is many phases away. (d) lifelong customer is the Retention output — last stage, not first.

questions.question: Which part of the training center team should be aware of internal and external marketing efforts?
questions.explanation: Marketing alignment is a whole-team responsibility: (a) Pros mention current promos to students during classes; (b) owners set strategy and budget; (c) retail + travel staff are the brand's face in everyday interactions. If anyone on the team doesn't know about an active campaign, customers get mixed messaging — undermines conversion.

questions.question: What is the purpose of positioning yourself as an expert to your customers?

options.a: They will only ask you about training opportunities
options.b: They will learn that the internet is the best source of advice
options.c: They will come to you for advice on training, purchases, and travel
options.d: They will know to look somewhere else for advice

questions.explanation: Expert status = first-call status across the entire diving journey: training program selection, gear purchases, dive destination recommendations, equipment service. Trust + competence creates a multi-touchpoint relationship where the customer keeps coming back. (a) limits expertise to one slice. (b) and (d) describe what happens when you LOSE trust — customer goes elsewhere.

questions.question: Which of the following is part of a targeted marketing strategy?
questions.explanation: Targeted marketing = aim at a specific audience efficiently with a clear value proposition. Cost-effective + consistent messaging both reflect that discipline (spend lands where conversion is highest, every touchpoint reinforces the same brand promise). (a), (c) and (d) include mass-emailing or cold calling — both are spray-and-pray UN-targeted techniques that contradict the strategy.

questions.question: Which of the following applies to the Acquisition phase?
questions.explanation: Three pillars of effective Acquisition: (b) welcoming environment — the first impression has to feel inclusive (mssi-inst-206); (c) expert positioning (mssi-inst-383) — trust in competence converts interest into action; (d) targeted marketing (mssi-inst-384) — right message reaching the right audience efficiently. Together they make Acquisition work.

questions.question: Why is creating a welcoming atmosphere a key component of the Acquisition phase?

options.a: It is not a key component of the Acquisition phase
options.b: Customers who do not feel comfortable will seek out a different training center
options.c: It convinces new divers to register for continuing education programs
options.d: It makes experienced divers less likely to become loyal customers

questions.explanation: Free-choice market (mssi-inst-380): uncomfortable customers don't complain — they just go to a competitor. Welcoming atmosphere removes the entry-barrier (intimidation, judgement, gear-snobbery vibes) and lets the conversation about training actually start. (a) inverts — mssi-inst-385 already confirmed it's a pillar. (c) con-ed enrolment is the Commitment phase, not Acquisition. (d) is nonsense.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of the Development phase?

options.b: To convince divers to purchase equipment

questions.explanation: Development phase = need-creation, consistent with the Diver Diamond philosophy (mssi-inst-224): show the diver all six components and let them recognise their own gaps — that recognition motivates the next program, gear, or trip. (a) lifelong-diver outcome is the Retention phase output, downstream. (b) equipment purchases are one expression of Development but too narrow as the primary goal. (c) all-correct sweeps in the misframings.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an orientation session?

options.a: To show off the most expensive equipment available at the training center
options.b: To demonstrate how experienced the dive professionals are
options.c: To highlight the features of the training center and its resources to a customer
options.d: To show the customer how complicated diving can be

questions.explanation: Orientation = a guided welcome through the whole TC ecosystem — classroom, retail floor, equipment service, travel desk, social events. The customer leaves understanding the full value proposition and what's available beyond the first program. (a) is hard-sell on the priciest gear — kills trust. (b) Pro-experience name-dropping = arrogance. (d) intimidating new customers about complexity destroys confidence.

questions.question: Why is it important for a training center to maintain a professional appearance?

options.a: It is more attractive to the customer, and builds confidence
options.b: The SSI Service Center can revoke membership if the training center is not well-maintained
options.c: Customers will only visit training centers with the most modern features
options.d: Training centers do not need to maintain a professional appearance

questions.explanation: Visual polish is a silent quality signal — clean classroom, organised gear room, branded décor all tell the customer 'these people run a professional operation; my safety and training are in good hands'. (b) over-formalises with a Service Center revocation threat — appearance isn't enforced that way. (c) 'only most modern' is elitist and false (well-maintained ≠ flashy). (d) inverts the truth.

questions.question: Which of the following is a technique for developing a relationship with your customer?

options.a: Encourage them to provide feedback and reviews
options.b: Demonstrate professionalism in appearance and training center activities

questions.explanation: Three relationship-building moves working together: (a) feedback / reviews — signals that the customer's voice matters and gives the TC data to improve; (b) professional appearance + delivery (mssi-inst-389) — silent quality signal that reinforces trust; (c) orientation sessions (mssi-inst-388) — guided introduction to the full value proposition. Together they form active investment in the customer relationship.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Development phase of the Customer Loyalty Cycle?

options.a: To seek out and find potential new customers
options.c: To encourage customers to become lifelong divers
options.d: To nurture and strengthen the relationship with your customer

questions.explanation: Development = middle phase of the Loyalty Cycle (mssi-inst-378). Acquisition is done — customer is already in. Now you nurture: deepen the relationship, build competence, surface their next-step needs (mssi-inst-387). (a) finding new customers belongs to Acquisition. (c) lifelong-diver outcome is Retention's payoff. (b) all-correct sweeps in out-of-phase activity.

questions.question: Reviews and training center/professional evaluations:

options.a: Help the training center create new travel and training opportunities
options.b: Make the customer feel like their opinion is valid

questions.explanation: Three concurrent functions of customer reviews + evaluations: (a) signal where to expand the offer (e.g. customers asking for drysuit specialty → run one); (b) customer voice validation — feeling heard builds engagement and trust; (c) blind-spot intelligence — surfaces operational issues the TC owners may not see. Together they form a continuous-improvement feedback loop.

questions.question: What is the most important consideration if you want to help a customer commit to your training center?

options.a: Only give them advice that creates more training opportunities for you to teach
options.b: Only give them advice that benefits your training center
options.c: Listen to their needs, and give them relevant advice
options.d: Listen to their needs, and advise them based on your opinions

questions.explanation: Customer-first approach: understand the customer's actual goals first, then steer them to programs / gear / trips that fit. Trust grows from 'this Pro listens to ME' rather than 'this Pro pushes their agenda'. (a) self-interested upselling. (b) manipulative TC-first framing. (d) opinion-based advice ignores the customer's specific needs — ego-driven instead of needs-driven.

questions.question: Why would a customer decide to travel with your training center?

options.a: They don't have any other options
options.b: They want an easy travel experience with a business they are already familiar with
options.c: They want you to sell them additional equipment and training
options.d: They don't know where else to find travel information

questions.explanation: Convenience + trust drive the decision: the customer already knows the Pros, the gear standards, the group dynamics — pre-trip stress and cognitive load drop dramatically vs. booking with a new operator. (a) frames the TC as a monopoly choice — false in any healthy market. (c) inverts motivation (customer's wish, not the TC's wish). (d) negative-reasoning framing — customers do their research; the TC has to actually be the better option.

questions.question: What should you consider when discussing equipment purchases with a customer?

options.a: You should recommend equipment that meets their needs and goals
options.b: They should buy the most-expensive equipment available in your training center
options.c: They should buy whatever brands you recommend

questions.explanation: Customer-first principle (consistent with mssi-inst-393): match gear to the student's actual diving plans — cold vs warm water, travel frequency, dive style, budget. Right-fit gear builds long-term trust + repeat purchases. (b) most-expensive hard-sell breaks trust on first transaction. (c) 'whatever brands YOU recommend' is arrogance + loss of customer agency. (d) all-correct sweeps in the manipulative claims.

questions.question: What is one way that you can connect continuing education to your customer's needs?

options.a: Register them for as many programs as possible
options.b: Emphasize that they should become an SSI Professional
options.c: Find programs that requires them to buy new equipment
options.d: Identify programs that will enhance their personal goals

questions.explanation: Same customer-first principle as mssi-inst-393, 395 — link education to the diver's own goals. They have a camera → recommend Photography; they're going on a Norway trip → Cold Water + Drysuit; they want to see wrecks → Wreck Diving. (a) 'as many as possible' is upsell anti-pattern. (b) pushing the Pro path is too aggressive on a recreational customer. (c) gear-tied programs as a sales tactic is manipulative bundling.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Commitment phase of the Customer Loyalty Cycle?

options.b: To create need in your customers, and reinforce their connection to your training center
options.c: To convince customers to buy as much equipment as possible
options.d: To bring new customers into the training center

questions.explanation: Phase 3 of the Loyalty Cycle (mssi-inst-378). Customer is past Development (relationship nurtured, competence built); Commitment moves them into invested-state — gear ownership, con-ed enrolment, community participation. Need-creation continues, emotional bond strengthens. (a) Pro path is a separate (and rare) outcome. (c) 'as much as possible' is the manipulative read — actual goal is fitting commitment, not maximising it. (d) new customers belong to Acquisition (phase 1).

questions.question: After completing the Retention phase of the Customer Loyalty Cycle, the customer:

options.a: Will be a customer for life
options.b: Restarts the cycle at one of the earlier phases
options.d: Does not require any additional effort from the training center

questions.explanation: Loyalty Cycle is a LOOP, not a linear endpoint (mssi-inst-378). Each new program triggers a fresh Development phase, each new specialty kicks off Commitment again — continuous engagement loop. (a) 'customer for life' over-states the static endpoint; the cycle has to keep moving or commitment fades. (d) 'no additional effort' is the inverted truth — Retention is the most active phase, requiring continuous attention. (c) all-correct sweeps in those static-endpoint claims.

questions.question: After passing the Instructor Evaluation, an Open Water Instructor can teach:

options.a: Open Water Diver, Enriched Air Nitrox, Diver Stress & Rescue, and Divemaster
options.b: Enriched Air Nitrox, Diver Stress & Rescue, and Divemaster
options.c: Open Water Diver, Perfect Buoyancy, Basic Diver and Science of Diving
options.d: Perfect Buoyancy, Open Water Diver, Dive Guide, and Deep Diving

questions.explanation: Comprehensive OWI scope post-IE in SSI's pathway: entry-level cert (OWD), gas-management specialty (EAN), rescue (S&R), and Pro-level training (Divemaster). The DM-teaching authority lands at OWI in SSI structure (vs other agencies where it sits higher, at AIT/IT). Distractors mix in specialties needing separate Specialty Instructor ratings (Perfect Buoyancy, Deep Diving — would need those SI certs) or programs outside this scope (Basic Diver, Dive Guide, Science of Diving programs aren't in this base bundle).

questions.question: How can you support a diver who is looking for new travel opportunities?

options.a: Recommend trips that require the diver to take additional training
options.b: Recommend trips that meet their goals and budget
options.c: Only recommend trips that have the highest profit margin for the training center
options.d: Recommend trips that will require the diver to purchase new equipment

questions.explanation: Customer-first travel advice (consistent with mssi-inst-393, 395, 396): match the trip to goals AND financial reality. Right-fit recommendations build trust + repeat travel through the TC. (a) bundling training as a trip prereq is forced upsell. (c) highest-profit-first is the manipulative TC-first frame. (d) requiring gear purchases as a trip filter is bundling for the wrong reason.

questions.question: People continue to dive because:

options.a: They have personal goals, enjoy dive traveling, or seek recognition
options.b: They enjoy spending their money on expensive hobbies

questions.explanation: Three driving motivators for long-term divers: PERSONAL GOALS (skill progression — Master Diver, photography mastery, tec ratings), TRAVEL (destinations they want to see — Egypt, Indonesia, the Pacific), RECOGNITION (logged-dive milestones, AOWD, MD cards, Century Diver). All three are positive engagement loops. (b) cynical mischaracterisation. (c) absurd. (d) all-correct sweeps in the bad framings.

questions.question: Which of the following is a method of retaining divers?
questions.explanation: Three retention strategies that work together: (b) personal touch — follow-up after class, remembering preferences, birthday-message-style attentiveness; (c) accessibility — flexible scheduling, gear rental, beginner-friendly entry points so divers stay active; (d) community building beyond the water — BBQs, club nights, fundraisers, social media engagement, fish-id parties. Together they form a community a diver doesn't want to leave.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Retention phase of the Customer Loyalty Cycle?

options.a: To get as many sales as possible before a customer stops diving
options.b: To keep your customers actively diving and engaged with your training center
options.c: To focus on attracting new customers to the training center
options.d: To keep your customers in the training center as long as possible

questions.explanation: Retention = the sustain phase of the Loyalty Cycle (mssi-inst-378, 398). Goal: the diver keeps actually diving + keeps coming back for programs / gear / trips / community events. Active engagement, not passive membership. (a) is defeatist + transactional. (c) is the Acquisition phase, not Retention. (d) 'as long as possible' is vague — physically in the shop? Mentally engaged? Active diving is the right metric.

questions.question: What is the surface air consumption rate of a diver with a 200 bar, 10-liter cylinder who uses 50 bar in 10 minutes at a depth of 10 meters?
questions.explanation: SAC (surface air consumption) is the gas volume used per minute referenced to surface pressure. Volume consumed at depth = 50 bar × 10 L = 500 L. At 10 m the absolute pressure is 2 ATA, so surface-equivalent volume = 500 L / 2 = 250 L. Over 10 minutes: 250 / 10 = 25 L/min.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: Which physical law states: "If temperature remains constant, the pressure and volume of any gas are inversely related"?
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: at constant temperature, pressure and volume of a gas are inversely proportional (P1·V1 = P2·V2). Dalton's deals with partial pressures in a gas mix, Archimedes' principle with buoyancy, Henry's with gas dissolution into liquids.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: Assuming a maximum partial pressure limit of 1.4 bar, the maximum operating depth for EAN32 is:
questions.explanation: MOD = ((PO2_limit / fO2) − 1) × 10 m. For EAN32 at 1.4 bar: ((1.4 / 0.32) − 1) × 10 = (4.375 − 1) × 10 ≈ 33.75 m, rounded down to 34 m. (At 1.6 bar contingency, MOD ≈ 40 m.)
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: If 2 liters of air are needed in your buoyancy compensator to maintain neutral buoyancy at 10 meters, what will happen to your buoyancy if you ascend 3 meters without deflating?

options.b: You will stay neutral no matter if you ascend or descend
options.c: There will be no change in buoyancy

questions.explanation: On ascent surrounding pressure decreases (10 m = 2 ATA → 7 m = 1.7 ATA), so air in the BCD expands (Boyle: V2 = V1 × P1/P2 = 2 × 2/1.7 ≈ 2.35 L). The diver now displaces more water → positive buoyancy increases, leading to accelerating ascent unless gas is vented.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: A diver and their equipment weighs 87 kilograms when neutrally buoyant in salt water. To be neutrally buoyant in fresh water with the same equipment, they must:
questions.explanation: Salt water (~1.03 kg/L) is denser than fresh water (~1.00 kg/L), so it provides more buoyant lift for the same displaced volume. Moving from salt to fresh water reduces buoyancy — to stay neutral the diver must REMOVE weight (typically about 2.5–3% of total system weight, ~2–3 kg for an 87 kg setup).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: At what depth does the partial pressure of oxygen in air equal 1.6 bar?
questions.explanation: Air is 21% oxygen. PO2 = fO2 × P_abs → P_abs = 1.6 / 0.21 ≈ 7.62 ATA → depth = (7.62 − 1) × 10 ≈ 66 m. This is the CNS oxygen-toxicity contingency limit; the working PO2 limit on air is 1.4 bar (~57 m).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: For every meter of depth in fresh water, pressure changes by:
questions.explanation: Fresh water density ≈ 1000 kg/m³. Pressure increase per metre = ρ·g·h = 1000 × 9.81 × 1 ≈ 9 810 Pa = 0.0981 bar. Salt water (~1025 kg/m³) gives ≈ 0.1006 bar/m, often rounded to 0.1 bar/m.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: The absolute pressure at a depth of 50 meters is:
questions.explanation: Hydrostatic pressure at 50 m in sea water ≈ 5 bar (50 × 0.1). Add 1 bar of atmospheric pressure → absolute pressure = 6 bar (6 ATA).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: Breathing gas consumption:

options.b: Remains the same as depth increases

questions.explanation: Gas density is proportional to absolute pressure. The diver's lungs deliver the same tidal volume each breath, but each breath now contains more molecules — so gas consumption (in surface-equivalent volume) rises roughly linearly with absolute pressure (e.g., at 30 m / 4 ATA you use ~4× the surface SAC).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 1 Diving Physics

questions.question: The cardiovascular system consists of these three major components:

options.c: Right atrium, left atrium and arteries only

questions.explanation: The cardiovascular (circulatory) system has three primary parts: the heart as the pump, blood as the transport medium for gases/nutrients/waste, and the blood vessels (arteries, veins, capillaries) as the plumbing.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: At sea level, human body tissues are saturated with nitrogen at a partial pressure equal to:

options.a: The absolute pressure of air at sea level
options.b: The hydrostatic pressure of water at sea level
options.c: The partial pressure of the gas in alveolar air
options.d: The gauge pressure of the gas in the lungs

questions.explanation: Tissues equilibrate with the partial pressure of nitrogen in the alveolar air (Henry's Law). On the surface that's roughly 0.79 ATA × 1 ATA ≈ 0.79 bar PN2. As ambient pressure rises with depth, alveolar PN2 rises and tissues on-gas; on ascent, the gradient reverses.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: There are several lung overexpansion injuries that you should be aware of so that you can avoid them while diving. These are:

options.b: Decompression sickness, arterial gas embolism, rupture of the oval window
options.d: Gas embolism, mediastinal emphysema, subcutaneous emphysema, pneumothorax

questions.explanation: The four classic pulmonary overexpansion (POIS) injuries: arterial gas embolism (AGE), mediastinal emphysema (gas in the chest cavity), subcutaneous emphysema (gas under the skin), and pneumothorax (collapsed lung). DCS is gas-bubble formation from supersaturation, not lung overexpansion. Oval window rupture is an inner-ear injury.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: The recommended method to avoid sinus squeezes and reverse blocks is to:

options.a: Ascend and descend slowly to allow gradual equalization
options.b: Forcibly equalize the pressure in your sinuses every few meters
options.c: Dive only when your sinuses are healthy and clear
options.d: Only use decongestants that do not produce side effects

questions.explanation: Sinuses cannot be voluntarily equalized — the only reliable defence is to dive only when sinuses are healthy and clear. Decongestants can wear off mid-dive causing reverse blocks on ascent; forced equalization can damage the inner ear; slow descent helps but does not solve a blocked sinus.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: Permanent hearing loss, vertigo and ringing in the ear are most likely to be the result of a ruptured:
questions.explanation: The round and oval windows separate the middle ear from the fluid-filled inner ear. Forceful Valsalva or rapid pressure differentials can rupture them, causing permanent sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus and vertigo (perilymph fistula). Eardrum rupture is painful but typically heals with much milder long-term effects; the Eustachian tube does not 'rupture'.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: A physiological condition called a squeeze occurs when:

options.a: The pressure on the inside of an air space is increasing to equalize with the external pressure
options.b: The pressure on the outside of an closed air space is greater than the internal pressure
options.c: The pressure on the inside of an air space is greater than the external pressure
options.d: None of the answers are correct

questions.explanation: A squeeze is barotrauma of descent: external (ambient water) pressure exceeds the pressure in a closed air space (mask, ear, sinus, suit), and the tissue lining is pulled into the space. The reverse — internal > external — is a 'reverse block' on ascent.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: If you have difficulty equalizing pressure in your ears or sinuses while descending, you should:

options.a: Ascend until the pressure is relieved and then attempt equalization
options.b: Stop and wait until the discomfort subsides
options.c: Return to the surface and re-initiate the descent
options.d: Ascend two meters and force equalization

questions.explanation: Standard procedure: ascend a metre or two until the pressure is relieved, then equalize gently and continue descending. Forcing or 'waiting it out' can drive a middle-ear or inner-ear barotrauma. Aborting to the surface is only needed if equalization repeatedly fails.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: The primary symptom of ear squeeze in its initial phase is:

options.b: A pressure that will progress to severe pain if equalization is not completed
options.d: A pulling sensation in the chest

questions.explanation: Initial ear squeeze presents as a feeling of pressure or fullness; if the diver continues to descend without equalizing, this progresses to severe pain and ultimately tympanic membrane rupture. Joint pain points to DCS, chest pulling to lung/mediastinal issues.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: A lung rupture can occur with over-pressurization in water as shallow as:
questions.explanation: Lung overexpansion injury can occur with as little as ~1.2 m (4 ft) of pressure differential when a diver holds their breath while ascending — the lungs cannot stretch more than a few percent before alveoli rupture. This is why the cardinal rule is 'never hold your breath'.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: Which of the following statements concerning heat stroke is not true?

options.c: Heat stroke is an extremely serious condition

questions.explanation: In classic heat stroke the body's thermoregulation fails and sweating typically STOPS — the skin becomes hot, dry and red. Profuse sweating is characteristic of heat exhaustion, the precursor stage. Heat stroke is a true medical emergency: rapid pulse, very high core temperature, altered mental status.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 2 Diving Physiology

questions.question: Tissue half-time is a method used to describe the time necessary to allow the amount of gas in a particular tissue to change by:
questions.explanation: Half-time is the time required for a tissue compartment to move halfway (50%) toward equilibrium with the ambient inert-gas partial pressure — whether on-gassing or off-gassing. After 1 half-time it has changed 50%, after 2 half-times 75%, after 6 half-times it is considered ~98.4% saturated.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: If a diver suffers decompression sickness in a remote location, they should:

options.b: Transport the diver to a treatment facility even if that involves considerable delay

questions.explanation: Field treatment for suspected DCS: 100% oxygen via demand or non-rebreather mask, oral fluids if the diver is conscious and not vomiting, lay them flat, and arrange transport to a recompression chamber — even if the journey is long, because the only definitive treatment is hyperbaric therapy.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: After a 2:45 surface interval as a group D diver, what is your new group designation?
questions.explanation: ⚠️ NEEDS VERIFICATION — confirm against the actual SSI Diver Tables. Tentative answer: A. Reasoning: a 2:45 surface interval starting from group D should drop the diver back to group A on standard SSI Tables, because by ~2 hours most groups have washed out the residual nitrogen attributed to the controlling tissue. The exact table cell varies by edition — cross-check before relying on this in an exam.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: On desaturation, the inert gas diffuses from the tissues into the bloodstream, from the blood into the alveolar air, and then:

options.b: Into the sensor of the dive computer
options.c: Out of the body with each exhalation
options.d: Through the lung tissues into the mediastinum

questions.explanation: Desaturation pathway: tissue → blood → alveolar gas → exhaled breath. Inert gas is eliminated with each exhalation; the rate is limited by perfusion of the slowest tissues, which is why decompression is graded.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: The term "fast tissue" refers to:

options.a: Slow tissues which will change on emergency ascents
options.b: The rate at which they absorb and eliminate nitrogen
options.d: The ascent rate of the diver if he is too fast

questions.explanation: 'Fast' vs 'slow' tissue refers to how quickly a theoretical compartment on/off-gases inert gas — i.e. its half-time. Fast tissues (e.g. blood, brain, ~5 min half-times) saturate and desaturate quickly; slow tissues (bone, fat, 240+ min) take much longer.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: Multi-level dives, in general, are those where:

options.a: Divers spend time at progressively deeper depths during the dive, which allows extended bottom time
options.b: Divers spend time at progressively shallower depths during the dive, which allows extended bottom time
options.c: All of the answers are correct
options.d: Divers spend time at progressively shallower depths during the dive, which allows less bottom time

questions.explanation: A multi-level dive starts at the deepest planned depth and progresses to shallower levels. Because tissue on-gassing slows at shallower depths, the diver can extend total bottom time relative to a square-profile dive. Going deeper as the dive progresses is the opposite of safe profile design.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: The recommended depth and time that a diver should spend at a safety stop on all dives is:
questions.explanation: SSI standard recreational safety stop: 5 m / 15 ft for 3 minutes (3–5 min is acceptable). It allows tissues a final off-gassing window and slows the last few metres of ascent where the largest pressure gradient exists.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: What would your minimum surface interval be if you wanted to make two dives to 18 meters for 30 minutes each?
questions.explanation: Per the SSI Diver Tables: 18 m / 30 min lands the diver in pressure group G. To repeat 18 m / 30 min the diver must be at residual-nitrogen group B or lower (so the second dive's adjusted no-deco time covers 30 min). Off-gassing from G down to that group requires a minimum surface interval of 2:29.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: The closer to the limits a computer has been pushed, the more:

options.a: Deep the diver will be on the next dive

questions.explanation: Decompression algorithms model probability, not certainty. The closer the diver runs to the no-decompression limit (or to a deco ceiling) the smaller the personal safety margin — physiology, hydration, fatigue and individual susceptibility can tip an 'allowed' profile into a hit.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: The process of desaturation begins if the diver:
questions.explanation: Desaturation (off-gassing) begins the moment ambient pressure drops below the partial pressure of inert gas in a tissue compartment — that's the start of ascent. Stops formalise the off-gassing, but the gradient reverses as soon as the diver begins ascending.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 3 Decompression Theory

questions.question: How does a gas-integrated dive computer help divers manage their gas?

options.a: By monitoring and displaying the remaining gas supply in real-time
options.b: By storing historical data about the temperature of the dive
options.c: By automatically adjusting the diver's breathing rate

questions.explanation: A gas-integrated computer reads tank pressure (wired or wirelessly via a transmitter) and shows real-time remaining gas supply, often combined with current SAC/RMV and gas-time-remaining estimates. It does NOT control the diver's breathing.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: The most common flags used to show the location of divers underwater are the:

options.a: Recreational diver flag and the Alpha flag
options.b: Recreational diver's flag and the beta flag
options.c: Alfa flag and the safe boaters flag
options.d: Beta flag and the look-out-below flag

questions.explanation: Two flags are recognised: the recreational/sport diver flag (red field with diagonal white stripe, mainly North America) and the international maritime Alpha flag (white-and-blue swallow-tail) which signals 'I have a diver down — keep clear at slow speed'.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: To avoid missing a dive, divers should always be prepared with a:
questions.explanation: The 'save-a-dive kit' is a small case of common spare parts (mask strap, fin strap, O-rings, mouthpiece, cable ties, batteries, defog, basic tools) that lets you fix the small failures that would otherwise scrub a dive. A whole second TDS is impractical to carry; the kit is the standard SSI recommendation.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: The thermal protection provided by wetsuits and dry suits can be enhanced by the addition of:
questions.explanation: Heat is lost fastest at the head, hands and feet. Adding a hood, booties and gloves dramatically improves overall thermal protection. Hot-water suits exist (commercial diving) but are not 'add-ons' to a recreational suit; helium is a worse insulator than air; back plates are buoyancy hardware.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: To replace the traditional weight belt, most modern buoyancy compensators have:
questions.explanation: Modern recreational BCs incorporate an integrated weight system: weights ride in releasable pouches in the BC itself, removing the need for a separate belt. The pouches are designed to be jettisoned with a single handle in an emergency.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: All cylinders filled with anything other than air must be:

options.d: Rented from an SSI Training Center

questions.explanation: Cylinders containing anything other than standard air (Nitrox, Trimix, oxygen, argon, etc.) must be clearly labelled with contents and analysis — green/yellow Nitrox tape, MOD sticker, max PO2, fill date — so no diver accidentally breathes the wrong gas at the wrong depth.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: The first-stage of a regulator:

options.a: Reduces high-pressure air coming from the cylinder to a consistent intermediate pressure
options.b: Reduces high-pressure air coming from the cylinder to an increased intermediate pressure
options.d: Increases high-pressure air coming from the cylinder

questions.explanation: The first stage steps cylinder pressure (200–300 bar) down to a consistent intermediate pressure (typically ~9–10 bar above ambient). The second stage then drops it to ambient on-demand for the diver to breathe. Balanced first stages keep IP constant as cylinder pressure drops.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: The most comfortable, convenient and enjoyable way to dive is:

options.a: With your own professionally-fitted Total Diving System

questions.explanation: SSI's core philosophy: a diver's own Total Diving System (TDS) — properly fitted mask, fins, exposure suit, BC, regulator, computer, instrumentation — provides comfort, familiarity and reliability that rental gear can't match. (Also a marketing message of the SSI dealer model, but pedagogically grounded.)
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: What weight systems can divers choose for offsetting buoyancy?
questions.explanation: Three common weighting options coexist: traditional weight belt (around the hips), integrated weight pockets in the BC, and trim weights (small ankle/back-mounted weights to fine-tune horizontal trim). Most divers use a combination.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: The Information System includes:

options.a: Submersible pressure gauges and depth gauges

questions.explanation: The 'Information System' in SSI's TDS framework groups every instrument that gives the diver data about the dive: SPG, depth gauge, dive computer (which today often subsumes both), timer, compass, and digital extensions like wireless transmitters and HUDs.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 4 Diving Equipment

questions.question: Soft bodied animals without "backbone" found in the waters of the world have a scientific category called:
questions.explanation: Invertebrates is the broad scientific category for animals that lack a vertebral column — sponges, cnidarians (jellyfish, corals, anemones), molluscs, crustaceans, echinoderms, etc. Cnidarians are a subset of invertebrates; diatoms are single-celled algae.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: Creatures capable of inflicting injury will do so only:
questions.explanation: Marine animals do not stalk divers. Bites, stings and strikes happen when the animal feels threatened, cornered or surprised — defensive behaviour. Respect distance, don't touch, don't corner, and 99% of incidents are avoided.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: Which plants make up more than 98% of the bulk of the plants produced in the oceans?
questions.explanation: Phytoplankton — microscopic photosynthetic algae drifting in the upper sunlit layer — accounts for ~98% of ocean plant biomass and roughly half of all photosynthesis on Earth. Seaweeds, seagrasses and mangroves are tiny by comparison; corals are animals, not plants.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: An incoming tide is called a ____ and an outgoing tide is called an ____.
questions.explanation: Flood tide = water rising / coming in. Ebb tide = water falling / going out. Slack water is the brief calm at the turn between the two — usually the safest time to dive in tidal zones.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: The stinging mechanisms of jellyfish are located:
questions.explanation: Nematocysts (stinging capsules) are concentrated on the tentacles, where they're triggered by contact to harpoon prey or deter predators. The mesoglea is the jelly-like body layer; the epidermis and gastrodermis are the outer/inner cell layers but carry far fewer stinging cells.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: If a diver is injured by harmful sea life, it is usually because of:
questions.explanation: Most marine-life injuries are diver-caused: ignorance (not knowing what's dangerous), negligence (not watching where hands and fins go), or aggression (chasing, touching, harassing). Education + situational awareness eliminates the vast majority.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: A knowledge of underwater terrain helps divers:

options.b: Navigate safely through waves and currents

questions.explanation: Reading underwater topography supports every part of a dive: predicting where marine life will be, anticipating current behaviour around walls and pinnacles, choosing entry/exit points, planning depth profiles and navigation legs.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: What is the primary function of seagrass beds in marine ecosystems?

options.a: They prevent all sediment from settling on the seafloor
options.b: They stop seahorses being consumed by predators
options.c: They generate waves and strong currents
options.d: They provide food and habitat for various marine species

questions.explanation: Seagrass beds are nursery habitats and food sources for an enormous range of species — turtles, dugongs, manatees, juvenile fish, invertebrates. They also stabilise sediment and sequester carbon, but their primary ecological role is food + habitat.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: Although all corals contain stinging cells, one group is the most troublesome to divers. These are called:
questions.explanation: Fire corals (Millepora — actually hydrozoans, not true corals) deliver a potent burning sting on contact, leaving welts and pain that can last hours. Brain, leather and soft corals are far less aggressive. Standard rule: never touch the reef.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: Sponges can be recognized by the presence of ____.
questions.explanation: Sponges (phylum Porifera = 'pore-bearing') filter-feed by drawing water in through countless small pores (ostia) and expelling it through larger openings (oscula). The visible holes are the diagnostic feature.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 5 The Aquatic Environment

questions.question: A valid Medical Participant Questionnaire (or valid medical statement) is required:

options.b: Prior to any open water training
options.d: Any time SSI training is conducted

questions.explanation: Per SSI General Training Standards, the medical questionnaire (or signed physician statement) must be on file before the student enters the water — this includes confined-water sessions, not just open water. Pre-certification is too late; pre-OW only is too narrow.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: As defined in the General Training Standards, every SSI Dive Professional must use:

options.b: A DSMB and surface emergency signaling device

questions.explanation: GTS requires the SSI Dive Professional to dive with at least all the equipment students are required to use, plus a personal dive computer, plus a DSMB and a surface signalling device — so all three statements together describe the minimum kit.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: Each student must have the appropriate Digital Training Kit (if available):

options.a: Only if they are not sharing with another student
options.b: Only if the instructor requires them to
options.c: Only if they are older than 18 years
options.d: For every program they participate in

questions.explanation: SSI uses a one-kit-per-student-per-program model. The Digital Training Kit is the official student material set — it ensures each student has direct access to current standards, manuals, video and review tools, regardless of what their instructor or training centre prefers.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: As defined by the General Training Standards, a certified assistant is:

options.c: Able to assist with in-water training and supervision

questions.explanation: A 'certified assistant' under GTS must be: (1) an SSI Dive Guide / Divemaster or higher, (2) in current/active status with SSI (renewed Pro membership, current insurance), and (3) qualified to assist with in-water training and supervision. All three apply.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: The Candidate Water Fitness Evaluation includes:

options.b: A full diver rescue with a surface swim in under 8 minutes
options.d: A 750-meter surface swim with mask, fins and snorkel

questions.explanation: Per SSI General Training Standards, the Candidate Water Fitness Evaluation requires a full diver rescue with a surface swim completed in under 8 minutes — a timed, real-load rescue/tow demonstration. The other options are wrong: 50 m underwater is OW-level, 20 min tread is non-standard (15 min is the SSI norm), 750 m mask-fins-snorkel doesn't match SSI's distance benchmarks.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: Required open water training dives must be completed:

options.a: Within 9 months of the last academic or in-water training session
options.b: Within 6 months of the last academic or in-water training session
options.c: Within 12 months of the last academic training session
options.d: Within 6 months of the last academic training session

questions.explanation: Per SSI General Training Standards, required open water training dives must be completed within 6 months of the last academic OR in-water training session — the clock starts from whichever was most recent. After 6 months, the student needs a refresher before completing the OW dives.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: If it has been more than 12 months since the last in-water session, an entry-level student:

options.b: Must complete a Scuba Skills Update
options.c: Must do a complete academic review

questions.explanation: After >12 months out of the water, an entry-level student does not need to repeat the whole course — they complete an SSI Scuba Skills Update (refresher) covering academic review, equipment check and confined-water skill practice before continuing or diving.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: The SSI Flexibility Rule:

options.a: Requires skills to be taught exactly as described in the instructor manuals
options.b: Allows professionals to develop their own teaching style
options.c: Allows professionals to reduce the number of training dives as necessary
options.d: Allows professionals to add skills not endorsed by SSI to their training prog

questions.explanation: The Flexibility Rule lets the instructor adapt teaching style, sequence and presentation to the student's needs and the local environment — but it does NOT permit reducing required content, omitting performance requirements, or adding non-SSI skills as part of the certification.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: Prior to beginning any training, each student must complete the:
questions.explanation: Before training begins, every student signs/completes: (1) the program-specific Completion Record, (2) the Medical Participant Questionnaire, and (3) the Responsible Diver Code (plus liability release). All three are mandatory paperwork — no in-water activity until all are on file.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: An SSI Professional must have access to:
questions.explanation: An active SSI Professional must keep current copies (or digital access) of the latest student materials they teach from, the latest instructor manuals, AND any additional standards/materials referenced for the programs they conduct. Outdated materials = standards violation.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: In the General Training Standards, the rationale for teaching students to pass their primary regulator is found:
questions.explanation: The 'SSI In-Water Training Philosophy' section of GTS is where SSI explains the WHY behind specific skill choices — including why students are taught to hand off their primary regulator to a panicked OOA buddy and switch to their own alternate. The other sections cover scope (How To Use), administrative procedures, or duty-of-care obligations rather than skill rationale.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 6 General Training Standards

questions.question: The maximum depth for emergency ascent skills training during any open water dive is:
questions.explanation: Per SSI General Training Standards, controlled emergency swimming ascents (CESA) and other emergency-ascent skills are practiced from a maximum depth of 9 m / 30 ft. The shallow start keeps the ascent slow enough to manage and minimises lung-overexpansion risk if a student fails to exhale.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: Student-to-instructor ratios:

options.a: Should not be reduced for any reason
options.b: Cannot be increased for any reason
options.c: Are determined entirely by the instructor
options.d: Are defined by the specific program standard

questions.explanation: Each SSI program standard defines the maximum student-to-instructor ratio (e.g. OW confined 8:1, OW open water 8:1 with options to extend with certified assistants, Try Scuba 4:1, etc.). Instructors may always REDUCE ratios at their discretion for safety, but never exceed the program-defined maximum.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: Once a student completes their academic and in-water training requirements:

options.a: Their certification must be processed within 7 days
options.b: They will automatically receive a digital certification card
options.c: A printed card may be ordered from the training center or regional SSI Service Center

questions.explanation: All three statements apply: the instructor/training centre must process the certification within 7 days, the student receives a digital card automatically in their MySSI account, and a physical printed card can be ordered as an optional extra through the training centre or SSI Service Center.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: Instructors may conduct SSI training programs if they are:

options.a: As long as they are affiliated with an SSI Training Center
options.b: In active or under review status

questions.explanation: Only Active-status SSI Professionals may conduct training. Inactive members cannot teach (membership/insurance lapsed); 'under review' status indicates a quality-assurance investigation that suspends teaching authority; affiliation with a Training Center alone is not enough — the personal Pro status must be Active.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: When can an SSI Divemaster being used as a certified assistant evaluate skills?

options.c: During open water training dives only

questions.explanation: Never. A certified assistant — including a Divemaster acting in that role — assists with logistics, supervision and safety, but skill EVALUATION is reserved to the instructor of record. The DM can demonstrate, position students, watch for issues, and report observations, but the pass/fail call on a skill is always the instructor's.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: Limits for specific program depths are found:

options.a: In the Open Water Diver Instructor Manual
options.c: In the program's specific standards/instructor manual

questions.explanation: Each SSI program (OW, Deep, Wreck, Cavern, Trimix, etc.) has its own specific standards / instructor manual that defines its own depth limits — 18 m for OW, 30 m for Deep specialty Level 1, 40 m for Level 2, etc. The GTS sets common rules but program-specific depths live in the program's own document.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: Procedures for issuing diver referrals and replacing lost cards are found in:
questions.explanation: Administrative procedures that apply across all programs — referrals (cross-instructor or cross-agency), card replacement, name changes, certification corrections — live in the General Training Standards under 'Certification Procedures', not in any single program manual.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: A written emergency plan is required:

options.b: For any location where training dives are conducted
options.d: Only if the instructor is not familiar with the dive site

questions.explanation: An EAP (Emergency Action Plan) must be written and posted/available for any location where SSI training dives occur — pool, quarry, ocean — regardless of how well the instructor knows the site. It includes nearest chamber, emergency contacts, evacuation route, communication procedures.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true about the SSI definition of a swim-through:

options.b: They do not need clearly visible entry and exit points
options.d: They can be used by entry-level students

questions.explanation: SSI's swim-through definition includes a maximum length of 10 meters. A swim-through is distinct from cavern/wreck penetration: short, both ends clearly visible, with no overhead-environment certification required. Options A, B, D are wrong — there's no 30 m minimum depth, both ends MUST be clearly visible, and entry-level students CAN traverse a properly defined swim-through.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: The Water Fitness Evaluation must be completed before entry-level students:
questions.explanation: The Water Fitness Evaluation (swim distance, water tread, comfort) is the gate to OPEN water training — it is performed in confined water as part of the early program and must be passed before the student progresses to any open water training dive. It does not have to precede the first pool session.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: As defined in General Training Standards > Diving Environments, the Indoor Diving program requires:

options.b: A minimum surface area of 100 square meters

questions.explanation: The Indoor Diving environment is defined by all three criteria: a minimum depth of 5 m, a minimum surface area of 100 m², and the requirement that the venue be an aquarium or purpose-built facility (not a recreational swimming pool). All three must be true for a venue to qualify.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 7 General Training Standards

questions.question: The Experienced Diver Test:

options.a: Requires proof of open water diving experience
options.b: Requires an academic and pool/confined water evaluation
options.d: Is for divers that have lost proof of certification or have non-traditional diving histories

questions.explanation: The Experienced Diver Test (EDT) is the SSI pathway for divers who claim equivalent training but cannot produce a recognised certification card — e.g. lost cards, military / commercial / non-recognised-agency backgrounds. It requires proof of OW diving experience, an academic evaluation AND a pool/confined-water skills evaluation before the diver is awarded an SSI certification.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: Which of the following is not found in the Open Water Specialties section of the standards?
questions.explanation: Dry Suit Diving is NOT in the SSI 'Open Water Specialties' section — it is documented under a separate environmental/equipment specialty grouping. Wreck Diving, Deep Diving, and Navigation are all listed under Open Water Specialties.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: An Indoor Diver may upgrade to Open Water Diver after completing all skill evaluations and:

options.d: No open water training is required

questions.explanation: An Indoor Diver upgrades to OW Diver after completing all skill evaluations plus 2 open water training dives. The indoor program already covers the academic + confined-equivalent training, so only a short OW component is required to bridge to a full OW Diver certification.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: All mandatory training dives for SSI Specialty programs must be:

options.b: Indirectly supervised if a certified assistant is used
options.c: All training dives must be directly supervised

questions.explanation: Specialty mandatory training dives must be directly supervised by the instructor in the water with the students. Indirect supervision (e.g. instructor topside) is not permitted on mandatory training dives — it's only allowed on adventure/experience dives where students are already qualified to dive at the depth/conditions.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: The minimum age for dives in the Advanced Open Water Diver program is:

options.c: Determined by the applicable specialty program

questions.explanation: AOWD is a recognition certification — students complete five specialty/adventure dives chosen from the SSI specialty catalog. Each component specialty has its own minimum-age standard (e.g. Deep is 15+, others 10+ or 12+), so the minimum age for each AOWD dive is set by the specialty that dive belongs to.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: During open water training dives, the Open Water Instructor may:

options.b: Indirectly supervise students during underwater skill evaluations
options.c: Indirectly supervise the dive if a certified assistant is present
options.d: Indirectly supervise navigation skills during Open Water Training Dive 4

questions.explanation: On OW Training Dive 4, students perform a buddy-pair underwater compass navigation pattern — the instructor remains nearby but does not have to swim with each pair (indirect supervision). Emergency-ascent skills, underwater skill evaluations and the dive as a whole all require DIRECT supervision regardless of assistant presence.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: The Try Scuba program may be conducted by an active status:

options.c: Anyone can teach a Try Scuba program

questions.explanation: Try Scuba is the lowest-barrier SSI introduction experience, and authority to conduct it is delegated down to Assistant Instructor in active status. A Divemaster or Dive Guide alone cannot lead Try Scuba — they need at least AI authority because actual skill instruction is involved.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: Students participating in the Equipment Techniques program must be at least:
questions.explanation: Equipment Techniques can be taken from Referral Diver level — the program teaches gear care, assembly, fitting and basic configuration choices, which is appropriate for someone who has completed OW academics and pool but has not yet completed all OW training dives. Lower-bar entry than full OW Diver requirement.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: If one or more students in a Scuba Diver program are under the age of 15, the maximum student-to-instructor ratio in the open water is:
questions.explanation: For Scuba Diver programs with any student under age 15, the OW ratio cap is 4:1 — and SSI does NOT permit expansion via certified assistants for this age tier. The fixed 4:1 reflects the higher supervisory load of younger students and the limited depth/skills of the Scuba Diver program.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: When can an uncertified Open Water Diver student participate in a Scuba Skills Update?

options.a: When they have completed all of the pool/confined water training dives from the Open Water Diver Instructor Manual
options.b: When they have completed all of the academic sections from the Open Water Diver Instructor Manual
options.c: When they have completed all open water training dives

questions.explanation: A student who has finished all four OW training dives but has not yet been issued the certification card (paperwork pending, gap longer than allowed) may take a Scuba Skills Update to refresh and continue. Completing only academics or only confined water without OW dives is not enough.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: During the Open Water Diver program, Open Water Training Dives 3 and 4 may only be conducted:

options.a: After all academic requirements and 2 pool/confined water sessions are complete
options.b: After all academic requirements are complete
options.c: After two academic and 2 pool/confined water sessions are complete
options.d: After all academic and pool/confined water requirements are complete

questions.explanation: OW Training Dives 3-4 require ALL academic requirements (Sessions 1-6) AND all pool/confined water requirements complete. Per SSI standards, Dives 3-4 specifically need Academic 1-6 + Pool 4-6, but in normal sequence Pool 1-3 are also already done by then (Dive 2 requires Pool 2-3, and Pool 1 must be done before certification), so 'all academic + all pool' is the practical reading.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: The maximum student-to-instructor ratio for open water dives in a Basic Diver program is:
questions.explanation: Basic Diver is a more limited entry-level program (shallower depth, fewer skills), and SSI applies a tighter supervision cap: 4:1 maximum in the open water. The full OW Diver 8:1 framework does not apply.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: If any students in an entry-level program are younger than 12 years old, the maximum student-to-instructor ratio in the open water is:
questions.explanation: For any entry-level student under age 12 in the open water, the maximum ratio is fixed at 4:1 — no extension via certified assistants is permitted. This stricter cap reflects the much higher supervisory load and the developmental stage of younger children.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: Before getting a Referral Diver certification card, a student must:

options.b: Complete an Open Water Diver final exam
options.c: Complete all pool/confined water sessions and skill evaluations from the Open Water Diver Instructor manual.
options.d: Complete all academic sessions and assessments

questions.explanation: Referral Diver = a student who has completed every OW Diver requirement EXCEPT the four open water training dives, so they can complete those at another SSI facility (e.g. abroad on holiday). To be issued the Referral card they must have passed: all academic sections, the OW final exam, and all pool/confined-water sessions and skill evaluations.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 8 Dive Program Standards

questions.question: Which of the following statements is not a requirement for the SSI Divemaster rating?

options.a: Have logged at least 60 open water dives
options.c: Pass the Science of Diving final exam
options.d: Have the Diver Stress & Rescue certification

questions.explanation: The minimum age for SSI Divemaster is 18, not 21. The other three are real requirements: Diver Stress & Rescue prerequisite, Science of Diving final exam, and 60+ logged open water dives.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 9 Dive Professional Standards

questions.question: Assistant Instructors may teach, supervise and issue certifications/recognition cards for the following programs:

options.a: Scuba Explorers, Try Scuba, Scuba Skills Update, and Deep Diving
options.b: Scuba Explorers, Try Scuba, and all SSI Specialty programs
options.c: Scuba Explorers, Basic Diver, Scuba Skills Update, and Open Water Diver
options.d: Scuba Explorers, Try Scuba, Scuba Skills Update, and Perfect Buoyancy

questions.explanation: AIs have direct teaching/certifying authority for the lowest-load programs: Scuba Explorers, Try Scuba, the Scuba Skills Update refresher, and Perfect Buoyancy. Deep Diving and OW Diver remain Open Water Instructor and above; not all specialties are AI-accessible.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 9 Dive Professional Standards

questions.question: Which of the following is not a prerequisite for the SSI Master Instructor Rating?

options.b: Have logged at least 250 open water dives
options.c: Train primarily SSI programs at all dive professional levels
options.d: Be an active status Assistant Instructor Trainer

questions.explanation: 150 certifications issued is NOT a Master Instructor prerequisite — the actual SSI MI cert count threshold is different (higher). The real requirements include 250+ logged OW dives, training primarily SSI programs at all professional levels, and being an active-status Assistant Instructor Trainer.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Instructor Pretest — Part 9 Dive Professional Standards

questions.question: How can a SSI Divemaster earn an Ecology Specialty Instructor certification?

options.a: Complete a Specialty Seminar or the Verification Application
options.c: Complete a Specialty Seminar, or co-teach the program
