(d) FOLLOW THE DIVE COMPUTER — modern algorithms (Bühlmann ZH-L16 + GF, RGBM, VPM-B) track all 16 tissue compartments in real time across multi-level profiles — much more accurate than square-profile tables. Computer enforces NDL, ascent rate, mandatory stops, surface intervals.

questions.question: How might gas bubbles enter arterial blood vessels?

options.a: Through a cut in the diver's skin
options.b: Pulmonary barotrauma or shunting through a patent foramen ovale

questions.explanation: Two recognized pathways for ARTERIALIZATION of bubbles. (1) PULMONARY BAROTRAUMA → ARTERIAL GAS EMBOLISM (AGE): a breath-hold on ascent or air trapping ruptures alveoli; gas crosses the alveolar–capillary barrier into pulmonary VEINS → left atrium → systemic arterial circulation, often lodging in cerebral arteries → 'stroke-like' symptoms within minutes of surfacing (most lethal dive injury, demands immediate O₂ + recompression). (2) PATENT FORAMEN OVALE (PFO) SHUNTING: ~25% of adults have a probe-patent flap between right and left atria. Under raised right-atrial pressure (Valsalva, coughing, heavy gear-strain, lifting tanks immediately post-dive), VENOUS bubbles — which would normally be filtered by the pulmonary capillary bed — can shunt directly into systemic arteries → cerebral, spinal, or cutaneous DCS even on conservative profiles. PFO screening is routine for divers with unexplained or repeated DCS. (a) Skin cuts don't introduce bubbles. (c) Tight wetsuit restricts venous return / contributes to IPE but doesn't 'arterialize' bubbles. (d) Vasoconstriction narrows vessels — doesn't move bubbles into arteries.

questions.question: What skin or lymphatic symptoms are indicative of decompression sickness?

options.a: Lack of motor control, personality changes, and disturbed vision
options.d: Amnesia, hearing loss, headache, and nausea

questions.explanation: Cutaneous (Type I) DCS — 'skin bends': (1) ITCHING — often the earliest sign, especially after chamber or saturation exposures; (2) RASHES — classic 'CUTIS MARMORATA': mottled, marbled, blue-red blotchy patches on torso, shoulders, abdomen (caused by venous gas in subcutaneous capillaries; often a marker of an underlying PFO and may herald more serious neurological DCS); (3) SWELLING — lymphatic obstruction by bubbles, gives 'peau d'orange' / lymphedema in groin, neck, axillae; (4) CREPITUS — palpable crackling under the skin = subcutaneous emphysema (free gas in tissue planes). Treatment: 100% surface O₂, oral hydration, evac to a chamber if cutis marmorata, spreading rash, or any neurological signs. (a) Motor / personality / vision changes are CEREBRAL Type II DCS. (b) Mixes skin (rashes) with CNS (confusion, unconsciousness). (d) Amnesia, hearing loss, headache, nausea = INNER-EAR / CEREBRAL Type II DCS.

questions.question: What is pulmonary barotrauma?

options.b: Lung damage caused by a traumatic brain injury
options.c: Tissue damage and possible gas leakage caused by an excessive pressure gradient
options.d: Tissue damage caused by oxygen toxicity

questions.explanation: PULMONARY BAROTRAUMA = lung tissue damage caused by an excessive TRANS-ALVEOLAR pressure gradient. Mechanism: ascent with closed glottis / breath-hold / locally trapped air (asthma plug, mucus, broncho-spasm) → alveolar gas expands per Boyle's Law → as little as ~80–100 mbar of ΔP across the alveolar wall ruptures it. Four resulting injuries (the 'lung overexpansion family'), increasing severity: (1) MEDIASTINAL EMPHYSEMA — gas in the mediastinum: chest tightness, voice change, neck fullness; (2) SUBCUTANEOUS EMPHYSEMA — gas tracking up to neck tissue, palpable CREPITUS under the skin; (3) PNEUMOTHORAX — gas in pleural space, lung collapses: sharp unilateral chest pain, dyspnea, decreased breath sounds (tension pneumothorax = surgical emergency); (4) ARTERIAL GAS EMBOLISM (AGE) — gas enters pulmonary veins → left heart → systemic arteries → brain (stroke-like symptoms within minutes of surfacing — most lethal dive injury). This is exactly why the #1 SSI rule is NEVER HOLD YOUR BREATH. (a) Confuses two unrelated findings — crepitus comes from subQ emphysema; cutis marmorata is cutaneous DCS. (b) Wrong causal direction. (d) Oxygen toxicity = gas-tox issue (CNS hit / pulmonary tox at high PpO₂), not mechanical.

questions.question: What are some of the symptoms of lung barotrauma?

options.c: Numbness in the hands and feet and ringing in the ears

questions.explanation: Hallmark presentations of pulmonary barotrauma — both elements in (a) are classic: HEMOPTYSIS (coughing up blood, sometimes pink-frothy sputum) from alveolar rupture and bleeding into the airways; LIGHTHEADEDNESS / DIZZINESS / NEAR-SYNCOPE arises when AGE has embolized cerebral circulation, or when a tension pneumothorax compromises cardiac output. Other markers in the same picture (so you recognize the full pattern): sharp chest pain on/after ascent, shortness of breath, voice change (mediastinal emphysema), neck fullness with palpable crepitus (subQ emphysema), and the dramatic AGE presentation — 'stroke-like' focal weakness, slurred speech, vision changes, loss of consciousness — within minutes of surfacing. Onset timing is the key clue: AGE shows up almost immediately, DCS more often 30 min – 24 h post-dive. (b) Bradycardia + vertigo is more dive-reflex / inner-ear, not lung injury. (c) Numbness + tinnitus suggests Type II DCS / inner-ear barotrauma, not pulmonary. (d) Dry mouth + nausea are non-specific post-dive (dehydration, seasickness).

questions.question: What is an arterial gas embolism?

options.a: The blockage of an arterial blood vessel by bubbles
options.c: The blockage of a vein by bubbles
options.d: Blue lips caused by low oxygen

questions.explanation: ARTERIAL GAS EMBOLISM (AGE) = blockage of an ARTERIAL blood vessel by gas bubbles. Pathway: pulmonary barotrauma (breath-hold ascent / air trapping) → ruptured alveoli → gas crosses into pulmonary VEINS → left atrium → left ventricle → pumped into the systemic ARTERIAL circulation → embolizes the organ wherever a bubble lodges (most often cerebral arteries → CEREBRAL AGE / CAGE). Symptoms onset is FAST — usually within minutes (often within seconds) of surfacing: focal weakness, slurred speech, vision loss, confusion, seizures, loss of consciousness. The most LETHAL diving injury. Treatment: lay supine (NOT head-down — that's outdated and worsens cerebral edema), 100% O₂, fluids if conscious, immediate evac to a recompression chamber. (b) A 'brain bleed' is a hemorrhagic stroke / SAH — vessel RUPTURE, different mechanism. (c) Bubbles in VEINS = VENOUS gas embolism (VGE) — commonly produced by every dive in tiny amounts, usually filtered out by the pulmonary capillary bed; bypass via PFO leads to arterialization (see mssi-sod-091). (d) Cyanosis is a SIGN of hypoxemia (could accompany severe AGE) but isn't the DEFINITION.

questions.question: Which predisposing factors can increase the risk of decompression sickness?
questions.explanation: All three are recognized SSI / DAN risk factors. (a) DEHYDRATION — reduces plasma volume, slows microcirculation, thickens blood, impairs N₂ elimination. The single biggest CONTROLLABLE risk factor (drink before / between / after dives, urine should run pale yellow). (c) OBESITY — adipose tissue has roughly 5× the nitrogen solubility of lean tissue (N₂ is lipid-soluble) AND fat is poorly perfused, so it loads slowly but unloads even slower → silent reservoir of dissolved gas that liberates during ascent and the surface interval. (d) EXTREME HEAT OR COLD — both disrupt the on-gas / off-gas balance: COLD during the bottom phase causes peripheral vasoconstriction (slows on-gassing — sounds protective), but late re-warming after surfacing causes peripheral vasodilation precisely when supersaturation is at its peak → bubble nucleation; HEAT post-dive (hot shower, hot tub, sauna, sun-baked deck) accelerates off-gas exactly when bubbles want to grow. Other classic risk factors not in this question: alcohol, fatigue, age, low fitness, hard exertion at depth, repeat-day diving, fast ascent, recent musculoskeletal injury, PFO, asthma.

questions.question: Which type of decompression illness involves the central nervous system?
questions.explanation: DCS is graded by severity: TYPE I (mild) covers MUSCULOSKELETAL 'bends' (joint/limb pain — most common presentation), CUTANEOUS (itching, cutis marmorata, rashes), and LYMPHATIC symptoms (peau d'orange swelling). TYPE II (serious / life-threatening) covers NEUROLOGICAL involvement — CEREBRAL (confusion, personality changes, vision/speech disturbance), SPINAL CORD (girdle pain, paraplegia, urinary retention — most-feared form, often misattributed to back strain initially), VESTIBULAR / INNER-EAR (vertigo, tinnitus, nystagmus, nausea — 'staggers'), and PULMONARY ('chokes' — right-heart bubble overload, retrosternal pain, dyspnea, cough). All Type II requires immediate O₂ + chamber. (b) Pneumothorax is a pulmonary BAROTRAUMA, not DCS. (c) Type I excludes CNS by definition. (d) ARTERIAL GAS EMBOLISM does affect the brain but is a SEPARATE DCI entity — barotrauma-driven, not bubbles formed from dissolved gas. DCI umbrella = DCS (this question) + AGE; this question specifically asks about DCS.

questions.question: Which condition can occur when gas enters the lining of the lungs?
questions.explanation: MEDIASTINAL EMPHYSEMA — a sub-injury of pulmonary barotrauma. When alveoli rupture, escaping gas tracks along the bronchovascular sheaths up into the MEDIASTINUM (the central chest cavity between the lungs that holds the heart, trachea, esophagus, great vessels). Symptoms: chest tightness, dyspnea, voice change, sometimes 'Hamman's crunch' (crackling synchronized with the heartbeat on auscultation), neck fullness as gas tracks further upward into subcutaneous tissue (subQ emphysema, palpable crepitus). Treatment: 100% O₂ (washes out N₂, accelerates reabsorption), rest, observation; rarely needs invasive intervention unless tension develops. Strictly speaking 'lining of the lungs' = pleura, and gas in the pleural space is PNEUMOTHORAX — but that's not on the option list, so mediastinal emphysema (gas in the adjacent compartment) is the intended answer. (a) Edema = FLUID accumulation in tissue, not gas. (c) 'Hypoxic myopia' is not a recognized term — myopia is short-sightedness, unrelated to lung gas. (d) Hypercapnia = CO₂ retention in BLOOD, a gas-toxicity issue, not gas in tissue.

questions.question: Visibility can be affected by:
questions.explanation: Visibility is influenced by weather, currents/tides, and other divers stirring sediment.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Weather conditions:

options.a: Always remain constant throughout the day
options.b: May change quickly and with little warning
options.c: Only change quickly in the tropical ocean regions

questions.explanation: Weather can change rapidly with little warning, which is why ongoing monitoring is part of the Dive Guide's responsibility.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Divemasters are responsible for:
questions.explanation: The Divemaster's core responsibility is the diver's well-being and safety; logbook updates and equipment assembly remain the diver's own responsibility.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What are the three components to establishing group control?

options.a: Be an authority figure, be prepared, and choose an appropriate group size
options.b: Be strict, uncompromising, and choose an appropriate group size
options.c: Be prepared, uncompromising, and let the divers determine the group size
options.d: Be an authority figure, be prepared, let the group decide what is best for themselves

questions.explanation: Group control rests on being an authority figure, being prepared, and choosing an appropriate group size.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What is a check dive primarily conducted for?
questions.explanation: A check dive is used to assess the diver's skills and comfort before more challenging dives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following dive parameters can only be determined at the dive site?
questions.explanation: Local weather and visibility can only be assessed accurately at the dive site itself on the day of the dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: If you or your divers are doing repetitive dives, you should:

options.a: Add an extra margin of conservatism for safety
options.b: Dive to the no-decompression limit on each dive
options.c: Plan decompression stops using your computer so you can continue diving
options.d: Schedule a rest day at least one every two weeks

questions.explanation: Repetitive diving accumulates nitrogen, so adding conservatism (shallower depths, shorter times, longer surface intervals) is the standard practice.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What equipment should be included on every dive?

options.a: First aid kit, save-a-dive kit, and backup Total Diving System
options.b: First aid kit, emergency action plan, save-a-dive kit
options.c: Emergency action plan, save-a-dive kit, and personal lights
options.d: Save-a-dive kit, backup Total Diving System, and personal lights

questions.explanation: A first aid kit, emergency action plan, and save-a-dive kit should accompany every dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Safety cylinders are used:

options.a: Only at the end of deep or wreck dives
options.b: Only in low-on-air situations or out-of-air emergencies
options.d: Only during difficult or high-current dives

questions.explanation: Safety cylinders are reserved exclusively for low-on-air or out-of-air emergencies.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: The purpose of a dive briefing is to:

options.a: Share information about the dive with your divers
options.b: Show your divers how experienced and capable you are
options.c: Ask your divers questions about their expectations

questions.explanation: The dive briefing shares essential information about the dive with the divers.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: During the dive briefing, you should:

options.a: Describe maximum and minimum depths only
options.b: Describe maximum depth limits and maximum dive time
options.c: Let the divers determine their maximum depths
options.d: Let the divers determine their depth limits and dive time

questions.explanation: The Dive Guide sets the maximum depth and maximum dive time during the briefing.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: To avoid overheating or fatigue, your diver should:

options.a: Put their equipment on at the same time
options.b: Only put their wetsuit on halfway
options.c: Leave their wetsuit unzipped during the briefing

questions.explanation: All listed practices help reduce overheating and fatigue before the dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Why should you conduct a pre-dive check?

options.a: It will prevent you from entering the water with faulty equipment
options.c: It ensures your cylinder valve is properly opened
options.d: It ensures your buddy understands your equipment configuration

questions.explanation: Pre-dive checks catch faulty equipment, confirm valves are open, and familiarize buddies with each other's gear.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: If a diver has an equipment failure:

options.a: Do whatever is necessary to get them in the water
options.b: Give them your equipment, and use theirs during the dive
options.c: Do not attempt repair without the proper training
options.d: Offer to be their dive buddy, and watch them closely

questions.explanation: Never attempt equipment repair without the proper training; refer to a qualified technician.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: A diver should enter the water with their mask:

options.c: Securely clipped to their buoyancy compensator

questions.explanation: Divers should enter the water with the mask on their face to avoid losing it on entry.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Verify remaining breathing gas supplies:

options.a: Only if a diver is low-on-gas
options.b: At regular intervals through the dive
options.c: At least once before starting the ascent
options.d: Every five minutes during the dive

questions.explanation: The Dive Guide should check remaining gas at regular intervals throughout the dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Dives deeper than thirty meters:

options.a: Do not require any special considerations
options.b: Should only be done in groups of four or less
options.d: Require divers to be alert for nitrogen narcosis

questions.explanation: Dives deeper than 30 m require divers to be alert for nitrogen narcosis.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Diving with a computer:

options.a: Allows a diver to plan limited decompression diving
options.b: Eliminates the need for dive planning
options.c: Accurately tracks bottom time, depth and nitrogen absorption

questions.explanation: A dive computer accurately tracks bottom time, depth, and nitrogen absorption, but does not replace planning.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: You must always _____, regardless of the dive type.

options.a: Plan a dive within the limits of your diver's experience and ability
options.c: Dive conservatively, and within the no-decompression limits
options.d: Ensure your divers' safety and well-being at all times

questions.explanation: All three principles apply on every dive: plan within divers' limits, dive conservatively, and ensure safety/well-being.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Once they reach the surface, divers should:

options.b: Keep a regulator or snorkel in their mouth

questions.explanation: On surfacing, divers should establish positive buoyancy, keep a regulator or snorkel in their mouth, and keep their mask in place.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Divers exiting to a boat should always:

options.a: Exit using any available ladder or rope
options.b: Stay well clear of the exit zone until they are ready to exit
options.c: Take their equipment off in the water

questions.explanation: Divers should stay clear of the exit area until they are ready to exit, to avoid contact with the ladder/boat.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Every debriefing should include:

options.a: A detailed review of the descent and ascent
options.b: A group discussion of the next dive
options.c: A list of the negative experiences from each diver
options.d: Depth, bottom time, and interesting underwater features

questions.explanation: A debriefing covers depth, bottom time, and notable underwater features so divers can update logs and reflect on the dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Regarding their attitude during emergencies, what is the key difference between a recreational diver and a Divemaster?

options.c: Divemasters are responsible for other divers

questions.explanation: The defining attitude of a Divemaster in emergencies is responsibility for other divers, not just for themselves.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: As a Divemaster, your rescue skills must be:

options.a: At a higher level than those of a recreational diver
options.c: Only as good as the strongest diver in the group
options.d: Equal to those of the divers in the group

questions.explanation: A Divemaster's rescue skills must exceed those of a recreational diver because they may need to assist others.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: An incident management plan provides:

options.a: Guidance on how to handle emergency situations
options.b: Detailed procedures for every possible emergency scenario
options.c: Only enough information to rescue a diver
options.d: Skill descriptions for conducting in-water rescues

questions.explanation: An incident management plan provides general guidance for handling emergencies; it cannot cover every possible scenario.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: When should you have an incident management plan?

options.a: Only when there are no instructors at the dive site
options.b: Only when the dive boat requires one
options.c: For any dive site where you work as a Divemaster
options.d: Whenever you are more than one hour from the training center

questions.explanation: Always have an incident management plan for any dive site where you work as a Divemaster.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: The dive site should have enough oxygen to at least:

options.a: Transport at least three divers to emergency medical care
options.b: Transport a diver to emergency medical care
options.c: Support a victim for a three-hour boat ride
options.d: Support at least two victims for a one-hour boat ride

questions.explanation: Minimum oxygen supply must be sufficient to transport a diver to emergency medical care.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following injuries is not caused by breathing compressed gas?
questions.explanation: Sea sickness is motion-induced, not caused by breathing compressed gas; the others are dive-related injuries.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Which injury is caused by gas trapped between the lung and the chest cavity?
questions.explanation: Pneumothorax is gas trapped between the lung and the chest cavity, collapsing the lung.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the primary consideration for treating a lung overexpansion injury?

options.b: Recompress the victim at five meters
options.d: Immediately fly the victim to emergency medical services

questions.explanation: Provide supplemental (high-concentration) oxygen as the primary first aid for lung overexpansion injuries.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: A victim of decompression sickness may:

options.a: Require recompression in a hyperbaric chamber

questions.explanation: Decompression sickness is treated by recompression in a hyperbaric chamber under medical supervision.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following is a general guideline for managing stress before the dive?

options.a: Only take divers on dives they are qualified for
options.b: Help everyone make a safe and thorough dive plan

questions.explanation: All three guidelines together help manage diver stress before the dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Eliminating stress before starting the dive:

options.a: Keeps it from becoming worse as the dive continues
options.b: Ensures the divers will have no problems once the dive begins
options.c: Means you do not have to watch the divers as close during the dive

questions.explanation: Resolving stress before the dive prevents it from compounding once the dive begins.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Stressed divers at depth may:
questions.explanation: Stressed divers exhibit all of these signs: comprehension problems, wide eyes, and rapid shallow breathing.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: A diver who is panicking:

options.a: Will describe their problem to their Divemaster
options.b: Has no control over their actions
options.c: Should only be assisted if they request help
options.d: Can usually correct the problem themselves

questions.explanation: A panicking diver has lost rational control over their actions and cannot be expected to self-rescue.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: If you observe a diver panicking:

options.a: Allow the diver to attempt to solve the problem first
options.b: Have their buddy provide assistance first
options.c: Take immediate action to correct the issue
options.d: Immediately remove the diver from the water

questions.explanation: A panicking diver cannot solve the problem themselves; the Divemaster must take immediate action.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Your priority when assisting a panicked diver at the surface is to:

options.d: Allow the diver to attempt to solve the problem first

questions.explanation: Establishing positive buoyancy is the first priority for a panicked diver at the surface.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Emergencies usually occur:

options.b: As the culmination of several smaller problems
options.c: To the entire dive group at once
options.d: Only to very new or inexperienced divers

questions.explanation: Emergencies are typically the result of several smaller, unmanaged problems compounding over time.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: The last-seen point is:

options.a: Always determined by the missing diver's buddy
options.b: The point where the underwater search should begin
options.c: Only important if multiple divers are missing
options.d: Only used for searches using rescue mode

questions.explanation: The last-seen point is the location where the underwater search begins.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Choose a search pattern based on:

options.b: The size of the search area

questions.explanation: Search pattern selection depends on the plan, the area size, and environmental/site conditions together.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: While you wait for emergency services to arrive, you should:

options.a: Leave the victim to signal the emergency services personnel
options.c: Stay with the victim and provide care
options.d: Report the incident to your training center

questions.explanation: Stay with the victim and continue providing care until emergency medical services arrive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Local authorities should:

options.a: Wait until emergency medical services arrive to take control
options.b: Allow the Divemaster to manage the scene
options.c: Not be involved with incident management
options.d: Take control of the scene once they arrive

questions.explanation: Local authorities take control of the scene as soon as they arrive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What should you record on the incident report form?

options.a: Every fact that you learn at the scene
options.b: Facts and opinions that you learn at the scene
options.d: Only information about the scene management

questions.explanation: Record only facts (no opinions) gathered at the scene on the incident report form.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: If you take possession of the victim's equipment:

options.a: Return it to the victim or their family as soon as possible
options.b: Record its condition and secure it in a safe location
options.c: Provide it to the emergency medical services personnel
options.d: Give a receipt to the local authorities if they request one

questions.explanation: Document the condition of the victim's equipment and secure it in a safe location for investigation.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: The purpose of the SSI Snorkel Diver program is to:

options.a: Develop new candidates for the SSI Freediver program
options.b: Provide an alternative to the SSI Scuba Diver program
options.c: Provide a basic introduction to snorkeling equipment and skills

questions.explanation: The SSI Snorkel Diver program offers a basic introduction to snorkeling equipment and skills.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: You will be issued the _____ certification after completing this program.
questions.explanation: Completing this program issues the Dive Guide certification (entry to the professional path).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What is required to upgrade to Divemaster?

options.a: Study the Science of Diving materials and pass the digital final exam
options.b: Complete 100 dives and pass the Ecology exam
options.c: Teach beginners and lead key programs
options.d: Log at least 50 dives and complete a specialty instructor course

questions.explanation: To upgrade Dive Guide to Divemaster, study the Science of Diving materials and pass the digital final exam.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What equipment should be included on every dive?

options.a: First aid kit, save-a-dive kit, and backup Total Diving System
options.b: First aid kit, emergency action plan, save-a-dive kit
options.c: Emergency action plan, save-a-dive kit, and personal lights
options.d: Save-a-dive kit, backup Total Diving System, and personal lights

questions.explanation: Every dive should include a first aid kit, an emergency action plan, and a save-a-dive kit so problems can be addressed in the field.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the primary consideration for treating a lung overexpansion injury?

options.b: Recompress the victim at five meters
options.c: Immediately fly the victim to emergency medical services

questions.explanation: Supplemental oxygen is the primary first-aid treatment for lung overexpansion injuries; flying or in-water recompression are not appropriate.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Your priority when assisting a panicked diver at the surface is to:

options.c: Allow the diver to attempt to solve the problem first

questions.explanation: Positive buoyancy is the first priority — once the diver floats safely, you can calm them and address the problem.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: An incident management plan provides:

options.a: Detailed procedures for every possible emergency scenario
options.b: Guidance on how to handle emergency situations
options.c: Only enough information to rescue a diver
options.d: Skill descriptions for conducting in-water rescues

questions.explanation: An incident management plan gives general guidance for handling emergencies; it is not an exhaustive script for every scenario.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: You will be issued the _____ certification after completing this program.
questions.explanation: Successful completion of this program awards the Dive Guide certification.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: When should you have an incident management plan?

options.a: Only when there are no instructors at the dive site
options.b: Only when the dive boat requires one
options.c: Whenever you are more than one hour from the training center
options.d: For any dive site where you work as a Divemaster

questions.explanation: Any site where you work as a Divemaster requires an incident management plan, regardless of distance or boat policy.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: What is a check dive primarily conducted for?
questions.explanation: A check dive is used to assess each diver's skills before leading them on more demanding dives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Diving with a computer:

options.a: Allows a diver to plan limited decompression diving
options.b: Accurately tracks bottom time, depth and nitrogen absorption
options.c: Eliminates the need for dive planning

questions.explanation: A dive computer accurately tracks depth, time and nitrogen loading, but does not replace dive planning.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: During the dive briefing, you should:

options.a: Describe maximum and minimum depths only
options.b: Let the divers determine their maximum depths
options.c: Describe maximum depth limits and maximum dive time
options.d: Let the divers determine their depth limits and dive time

questions.explanation: The briefing must establish the dive's maximum depth and dive time so the entire group dives within agreed limits.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: The last-seen point is:

options.a: The point where the underwater search should begin
options.b: Always determined by the missing diver's buddy
options.c: Only important if multiple divers are missing
options.d: Only used for searches using rescue mode

questions.explanation: The last-seen point is the location where an underwater search for a missing diver should begin.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Once they reach the surface, divers should:

options.c: Keep a regulator or snorkel in their mouth

questions.explanation: On the surface divers should establish positive buoyancy, keep a regulator or snorkel in their mouth, and keep their mask on.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: A diver who is panicking:

options.a: Will describe their problem to their Divemaster
options.b: Has no control over their actions
options.c: Should only be assisted if they request help
options.d: Can usually correct the problem themselves

questions.explanation: A panicked diver loses rational control and cannot self-rescue — immediate intervention is required.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Stressed divers at depth may:
questions.explanation: Stressed divers commonly show all of these signs: poor signal comprehension, wide eyes, and rapid shallow breathing.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: What is required to upgrade to Divemaster?

options.a: Complete 100 dives and pass the Ecology exam
options.b: Study the Science of Diving materials and pass the digital final exam
options.c: Teach beginners and lead key programs
options.d: Log at least 50 dives and complete a specialty instructor course

questions.explanation: Upgrading from Dive Guide to Divemaster requires the Science of Diving materials and passing the digital final exam.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following injuries is not caused by breathing compressed gas?
questions.explanation: Sea sickness is motion-induced and not caused by breathing compressed gas; the others are pressure-related diving injuries.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: A diver should enter the water with their mask:

options.d: Securely clipped to their buoyancy compensator

questions.explanation: The mask should be on the face during entry so the diver is ready to dive immediately and to prevent loss.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: As a Divemaster, your rescue skills must be:

options.b: Only as good as the strongest diver in the group
options.c: At a higher level than those of a recreational diver
options.d: Equal to those of the divers in the group

questions.explanation: A Divemaster must perform rescue skills at a level higher than recreational divers because they are responsible for divers' safety.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: The dive site should have enough oxygen to at least:

options.a: Transport at least three divers to emergency medical care
options.b: Support a victim for a three-hour boat ride
options.c: Support at least two victims for a one-hour boat ride
options.d: Transport a diver to emergency medical care

questions.explanation: On-site oxygen supplies must be sufficient to transport a victim to emergency medical care.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: While you wait for emergency services to arrive, you should:

options.a: Leave the victim to signal the emergency services personnel
options.b: Stay with the victim and provide care
options.d: Report the incident to your training center

questions.explanation: Stay with the victim providing continuous care until emergency services arrive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Verify remaining breathing gas supplies:

options.a: Only if a diver is low-on-gas
options.b: At least once before starting the ascent
options.c: Every five minutes during the dive
options.d: At regular intervals through the dive

questions.explanation: Gas supplies should be checked at regular intervals throughout the dive, not just before the ascent.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Choose a search pattern based on:

options.b: The size of the search area

questions.explanation: Search-pattern choice depends on the plan, the size of the search area, and the environmental conditions.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Why should you conduct a pre-dive check?

options.a: It will prevent you from entering the water with faulty equipment
options.c: It ensures your cylinder valve is properly opened
options.d: It ensures your buddy understands your equipment configuration

questions.explanation: Pre-dive checks confirm equipment works, the cylinder is open, and buddies understand each other's gear.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: The purpose of the SSI Snorkel Diver program is to:

options.a: Develop new candidates for the SSI Freediver program
options.b: Provide an alternative to the SSI Scuba Diver program
options.c: Provide a basic introduction to snorkeling equipment and skills

questions.explanation: The Snorkel Diver program is a basic introduction to snorkeling equipment and skills.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Local authorities should:

options.a: Wait until emergency medical services arrive to take control
options.b: Take control of the scene once they arrive
options.c: Allow the Divemaster to manage the scene
options.d: Not be involved with incident management

questions.explanation: Once local authorities arrive, they take control of the incident scene.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: If a diver has an equipment failure:

options.a: Do whatever is necessary to get them in the water
options.b: Give them your equipment, and use theirs during the dive
options.c: Offer to be their dive buddy, and watch them closely
options.d: Do not attempt repair without the proper training

questions.explanation: Never attempt to repair equipment without the proper training — that ensures safety and avoids further damage.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: You must always _____, regardless of the dive type.

options.a: Plan a dive within the limits of your diver's experience and ability
options.b: Dive conservatively, and within the no-decompression limits
options.d: Ensure your divers' safety and well-being at all times

questions.explanation: On any dive you must plan within your divers' limits, dive conservatively within NDLs, and ensure their safety.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Divers exiting to a boat should always:

options.a: Stay well clear of the exit zone until they are ready to exit
options.b: Exit using any available ladder or rope
options.c: Take their equipment off in the water

questions.explanation: Divers should stay clear of the exit zone until ready, to avoid being struck by the boat or other divers.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: The purpose of a dive briefing is to:

options.a: Share information about the dive with your divers
options.b: Show your divers how experienced and capable you are
options.c: Ask your divers questions about their expectations

questions.explanation: The briefing exists to share necessary information about the dive with the divers.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Eliminating stress before starting the dive:

options.a: Ensures the divers will have no problems once the dive begins
options.b: Means you do not have to watch the divers as close during the dive
options.c: Keeps it from becoming worse as the dive continues

questions.explanation: Pre-dive stress that is not eliminated tends to escalate during the dive, so resolving it early prevents bigger issues.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the first part of the Responsible Diver Code?

options.a: Evaluate the conditions before every dive, and make sure they fit my personal capabilities
options.b: Dive within the limits of my ability and training
options.c: Accept responsibility for my personal well-being on every dive
options.d: Be environmentally conscious on every dive

questions.explanation: The first item of the Responsible Diver Code is to dive within the limits of your ability and training.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Weather conditions:

options.a: Always remain constant throughout the day
options.b: Only change quickly in the tropical ocean regions
options.c: May change quickly and with little warning

questions.explanation: Weather can shift rapidly with little warning and must be monitored continuously.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: If you or your divers are doing repetitive dives, you should:

options.a: Dive to the no-decompression limit on each dive
options.b: Add an extra margin of conservatism for safety
options.c: Plan decompression stops using your computer so you can continue diving
options.d: Schedule a rest day at least one every two weeks

questions.explanation: Repetitive diving increases risk; add an extra margin of conservatism rather than pushing limits.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: If you take possession of the victim's equipment:

options.a: Return it to the victim or their family as soon as possible
options.b: Provide it to the emergency medical services personnel
options.c: Record its condition and secure it in a safe location
options.d: Give a receipt to the local authorities if they request one

questions.explanation: Document the equipment's condition and secure it — it may be evidence and must not be tampered with.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Dive Guides can:

options.a: Lead certified divers, like their friends and family
options.b: Act as a dive instructor for certified divers
options.c: Issue certifications for SSI Scuba Diver programs

questions.explanation: Dive Guides may lead certified divers but cannot teach courses or issue scuba certifications.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: What are the three components to establishing group control?

options.a: Be strict, uncompromising, and choose an appropriate group size
options.b: Be prepared, uncompromising, and let the divers determine the group size
options.c: Be an authority figure, be prepared, let the group decide what is best for themselves
options.d: Be an authority figure, be prepared, and choose an appropriate group size

questions.explanation: Group control rests on being an authority figure, being prepared, and choosing an appropriate group size.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Regarding their attitude during emergencies, what is the key difference between a recreational diver and a Divemaster?

options.a: Divemasters are responsible for safety for other divers

questions.explanation: The defining attitude difference is that the Divemaster is responsible for the safety of other divers.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: If you observe a diver panicking:

options.a: Allow the diver to attempt to solve the problem first
options.b: Take immediate action to correct the issue
options.c: Have their buddy provide assistance first
options.d: Immediately remove the diver from the water

questions.explanation: Panic requires immediate action — a panicked diver cannot self-rescue.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: To avoid overheating or fatigue, your diver should:

options.a: Put their equipment on at the same time
options.c: Only put their wetsuit on halfway
options.d: Leave their wetsuit unzipped during the briefing

questions.explanation: All three pre-dive prep tactics help prevent overheating + fatigue: (a) put equipment on at same time as buddy/group so nobody stands gear-up in the heat alone; (c) wetsuit on only halfway until water entry; (d) leave wetsuit unzipped during the briefing for cooling/ventilation. All three combined are SSI Dive Guide best practice in tropical conditions. Mirror of dg-exam-a-015 which uses 'All answers are correct' verbatim — Form B's red-circled key was misread as (c) during PDF extraction; user-confirmed correction.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Every debriefing should include:

options.a: A detailed review of the descent and ascent
options.b: A group discussion of the next dive
options.c: Depth, bottom time, and interesting underwater features
options.d: A list of the negative experiences from each diver

questions.explanation: A debriefing should cover depth, bottom time, and interesting features of the dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Divemasters are responsible for:
questions.explanation: The Divemaster's core responsibility is the diver's well-being and safety; logbooks and assembly are the diver's job.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Emergencies usually occur:

options.b: As the culmination of several smaller problems
options.c: To the entire dive group at once
options.d: Only to very new or inexperienced divers

questions.explanation: Most emergencies are the result of several small unresolved problems chaining together.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: What should you do before visiting a well-known dive site?

options.a: Only ask the boat captain what to expect
options.b: Review any available information on the site
options.c: Nothing if you know the site well
options.d: Ask your divers what to expect at the site

questions.explanation: Always review available information on the site, even one you know well — conditions and hazards change.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following dive parameters can only be determined at the dive site?
questions.explanation: Local weather and visibility cannot be reliably known until you arrive at the dive site.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Which injury is caused by gas trapped between the lung and the chest cavity?
questions.explanation: Pneumothorax is gas trapped between the lung and the chest cavity (in the pleural space).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Dives deeper than thirty meters:

options.a: Do not require any special considerations
options.b: Should only be done in groups of four or less
options.d: Require divers to be alert for nitrogen narcosis

questions.explanation: Below 30 m the risk of nitrogen narcosis becomes significant and divers must be alert for it.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: What should you record on the incident report form?

options.a: Every fact that you learn at the scene
options.b: Facts and opinions that you learn at the scene
options.d: Only information about the scene management

questions.explanation: Record every fact learned at the scene — opinions and conclusions are not appropriate on an incident report.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: A victim of decompression sickness may:

options.b: Require recompression in a hyperbaric chamber

questions.explanation: DCS treatment usually requires recompression in a hyperbaric chamber under medical supervision.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following is a general guideline for managing stress before the dive?

options.a: Only take divers on dives they are qualified for
options.b: Help everyone make a safe and thorough dive plan

questions.explanation: Pre-dive stress is managed by qualifying divers, planning thoroughly, and keeping a positive atmosphere — all of these.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Safety cylinders are used:

options.a: Only at the end of deep or wreck dives
options.b: Only in low-on-air situations or out-of-air emergencies
options.d: Only during difficult or high-current dives

questions.explanation: Safety cylinders exist for low-on-air or out-of-air emergencies, not as a routine deep-dive accessory.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: Visibility can be affected by:
questions.explanation: Visibility depends on weather, other divers stirring sediment, and currents/tides — all of these.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the minimum age for certification as an Open Water Diver?
questions.explanation: Minimum age for OW Diver (Junior) is 10.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: How many academic sessions does the Open Water Diver program have, as outlined in the instructor manual and training standards?
questions.explanation: OW Diver = 6 academic sessions.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: How many open water training dives does the Open Water Diver program have, as outlined in the instructor manual and training standards?
questions.explanation: OW Diver = 4 open water training dives.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: The combined bottom time for the Open Water Diver program's open water training dives must be at least:
questions.explanation: Combined OW dive bottom time min = 80 minutes.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: 13-year old Open Water Diver students must stay shallower than _____ during Open Water Training Dive 3?
questions.explanation: OW Training Dive 3 depth limit for 13-year-olds is 18 m.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the in-water ratio for open water training during the Open Water Diver program for students who are older than 15 years?
questions.explanation: OW dives 15+ = 8:1 max ratio.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the in-water ratio for open water training with one certified assistant during the Open Water Diver program for students who are 13 years old?
questions.explanation: 13-yr-old OW with 1 cert assistant = 6:2.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: If dry suits are used during open water diver training, the Open Water Instructor directly supervising the program must also:

options.a: Have a student-level dry suit certification
options.b: Be an active status SSI Dry Suit Diving Instructor
options.c: Be a Dry Suit Diving Instructor from any agency
options.d: Have at least five logged dry suit dives

questions.explanation: Active SSI Dry Suit Diving Instructor required.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: Open Water Training Dives 1 and 2 from the Open Water Diver program may be completed:
questions.explanation: OW Dives 1-2 allowed in indoor diving facilities.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: The Student Water Fitness Evaluation must be completed before:
questions.explanation: Water Fitness Eval before any OW training dives.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: How many students can be supervised by a certified assistant during an excursion dive in the Open Water Diver program?

options.b: Certified assistants are not allowed to supervise students

questions.explanation: Cert assistant on excursion = 2 students max.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: Junior Open Water Divers must dive under the _____ supervision of a _____.

options.a: Indirect, dive professional or adult Scuba Diver
options.c: Indirect, Dive Guide or adult Stress & Rescue diver
options.d: Direct, dive professional or adult Open Water Diver

questions.explanation: Junior OW: direct supervision by dive pro or adult OW Diver.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: An active status _____ must introduce and _____ supervise all emergency ascent skills during in-water training.
questions.explanation: Active OWI must introduce and DIRECTLY supervise emergency ascent skills.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: Who can supervise surface skills during the open water training dives if indirectly supervised by an Open Water Instructor?
questions.explanation: Active AI supervises surface skills under indirect OWI supervision.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: Who can supervise all open water training dives during an Open Water Diver program?

options.a: An Open Water Instructor in any status
options.d: An active status Open Water Instructor

questions.explanation: Active OWI supervises all OW dives.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: The Scuba Diver program is:

options.a: The academics and pool/confined water sessions from the Open Water Diver program
options.b: A prerequisite for the Open Water Diver certification
options.c: Required to become an autonomous diver
options.d: The first half of the Open Water Diver program academics and in-water training

questions.explanation: Scuba Diver = first half of OW academics + in-water training.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: Students completing the Scuba Diver program must finish:

options.a: Academic Sections 1–3, Pool/Confined Water Sessions 1–3, Open Water Training Dives 1–2, the Scuba Diver final exam
options.b: Academic Sections 1–6, Pool/Confined Water Sessions 1–6, Open Water Training Dives 1–2, the Scuba Diver final exam
options.c: Academic sections 1–3, pool/confined water sessions 1–6, open water training dives 1–4, Scuba Diver final exam
options.d: Academic Sections 1–3, Pool/Confined Water Sessions 1–3, Open Water Training Dives 1–4, the Open Water Diver final exam

questions.explanation: Scuba Diver: Academic 1-3 + Pool 1-3 + OW Dives 1-2 + Scuba Diver final.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the maximum depth limit for certified Scuba Divers?
questions.explanation: Scuba Diver max depth = 12 m.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: How can a Scuba Diver upgrade to an Open Water Diver?

options.b: Complete Academic Sessions 4–6 from the Open Water Diver program and the Open Water Diver final exam
options.c: Complete Pool/Confined Water Sessions 4–6 from the Open Water Diver program
options.d: Complete Open Water Training Dives 3–4 from the Open Water Diver program

questions.explanation: All three steps required for upgrade.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: The Perfect Buoyancy specialty program is a(n):
questions.explanation: Perfect Buoyancy is a Pool/Confined Water Specialty.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: A diver must be at least a(n) _____ before issuing the Perfect Buoyancy certification.
questions.explanation: Must be at least OW Diver before issuing Perfect Buoyancy cert.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: What are the completion requirements for the Perfect Buoyancy specialty program?

options.a: Complete all academic sessions and assessments from the instructor manual for Perfect Buoyancy
options.d: Complete at least two (2) in-water training sessions from the instructor manual for Perfect Buoyancy

questions.explanation: All three (academic + exam + in-water) required.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the open water depth limit for a 13-year-old student in the Perfect Buoyancy program?
questions.explanation: 13-yr-old Perfect Buoyancy OW depth = 18 m.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: How many in-water sessions are required for the Perfect Buoyancy specialty program?
questions.explanation: Perfect Buoyancy = 2 in-water sessions.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: Try Scuba participants will complete a(n):

options.a: Oral final exam and an experience dive in a pool/confined water environment
options.b: Experience dive in a pool/confined water environment
options.c: Experience dive in a pool/confined water environment and one fun dive in an open water environment
options.d: Mandatory skill evaluation in a pool/confined water environment

questions.explanation: Try Scuba = experience dive in pool/confined water.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: Is the SSI Open Water Diver program certified by ISO?
questions.explanation: OW Diver IS ISO-certified.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form A

questions.question: Can the Perfect Buoyancy program be conducted entirely in an open water environment?
questions.explanation: Yes, can be conducted entirely in OW.

questions.question: Can the Perfect Buoyancy program be combined with the Open Water Diver program?
questions.explanation: Yes, can be combined.

questions.question: What is the minimum age for the Try Scuba program?
questions.explanation: Try Scuba minimum age = 8 years old.

questions.explanation: Try Scuba max depth = 5 m.

options.d: An active status Scuba Explorer Instructor

options.c: Complete at least two (2) in-water training sessions from the instructor manual for Perfect Buoyancy

questions.explanation: All three (academic + exam + in-water).
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: How many students can be supervised by a certified assistant during an excursion dive in the Open Water Diver program?

options.c: Certified assistants are not allowed to supervise students

questions.explanation: Two students.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the minimum age for the Try Scuba program?
questions.explanation: 8 years old.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: How many academic sessions does the Open Water Diver program have, as outlined in the instructor manual and training standards?
questions.explanation: 6 academic sessions.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the open water depth limit for a 13-year-old student in the Perfect Buoyancy program?
questions.explanation: 12 meters.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the minimum rating to qualify for Ecology Specialty Instructor?
questions.explanation: Divemaster is the minimum rating to qualify for Ecology Specialty Instructor (DMs can earn it via Specialty Seminar or co-teaching). AI is below the threshold for this specific specialty.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: The lead instructor in the co-teaching method must be a(n):

options.a: Open Water Instructor and Specialty Instructor for the applicable program
options.b: Specialty Instructor who has issued at least five of the applicable certification
options.c: Specialty Instructor for the applicable program
options.d: Open Water Instructor with the applicable student-level certification

questions.explanation: OWI + Specialty Instructor.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following is part of the SSI Explorer program?
questions.explanation: All three.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: The combined bottom time for the Open Water Diver program's open water training dives must be at least:
questions.explanation: 80 minutes.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: Students completing the Scuba Diver program must finish:

options.a: Academic Sections 1–6, Pool/Confined Water Sessions 1–6, Open Water Training Dives 1–2, the Scuba Diver final exam
options.b: Academic Sections 1–3, Pool/Confined Water Sessions 1–3, Open Water Training Dives 1–2, the Scuba Diver final exam
options.c: Academic sections 1–3, pool/confined water sessions 1–6, open water training dives 1–4, Scuba Diver final exam
options.d: Academic Sections 1–3, Pool/Confined Water Sessions 1–3, Open Water Training Dives 1–4, the Open Water Diver final exam

questions.explanation: Sec 1-3 + Pool 1-3 + OW 1-2 + Scuba final.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the minimum professional certification to become a Computer Diving Instructor?
questions.explanation: AI.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: 13-year old Open Water Diver students must stay shallower than _____ during Open Water Training Dive 3?
questions.explanation: 18 m for 13 yo on OW Dive 3.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: The Perfect Buoyancy specialty program is a(n):
questions.explanation: Pool/Confined Water Specialty.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: A diver must be at least a(n) _____ before issuing the Perfect Buoyancy certification.
questions.explanation: OW Diver.
questions.source: Personal: ITC Scuba Exam Form B

questions.question: The term Surface Interval is defined as:

options.a: The amount of time spent on the surface of the water between dives (excludes time out of the water)
options.b: The amount of time the diver stays out of the water or on the surface between dives
options.c: Travel time to and from the dive site
options.d: The amount of nitrogen expelled while on the surface

questions.explanation: A Surface Interval is the total time a diver spends out of the water or at the surface between two dives. This time allows residual nitrogen to off-gas before the next dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The most serious lung overexpansion injury is:
questions.explanation: Arterial Gas Embolism (AGE) is the most serious lung overexpansion injury because air bubbles enter the arterial circulation and can block blood flow to the brain, causing stroke-like symptoms or death.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Diving at altitude or flying after diving:

options.a: Does not require any special considerations for the diver
options.b: Allows the diver to spend a greater amount of time at depth without exceeding the no-decompression limits
options.c: Allows the diver to ignore the no-decompression limits
options.d: Requires special diving computer functions, altitude tables and/or refraining from flying from at least 24 hours after the completion of a dive

questions.explanation: Reduced atmospheric pressure at altitude or in a pressurized cabin can cause residual nitrogen to form bubbles. Divers must use altitude-corrected tables/computer settings and wait at least 24 hours before flying.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: If a diver is injured by harmful sea life, it is usually because of:
questions.explanation: Most marine life injuries are defensive reactions provoked by divers through negligence, ignorance, or aggressive behaviour. Respecting marine life and maintaining good buoyancy prevents almost all such incidents.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the statements concerning sound transmission underwater is correct?

options.a: Divers find it difficult to locate the direction of a sound producing source
options.c: Sound will travel farther underwater than in air
options.d: Sound travels four times faster underwater than in air

questions.explanation: Underwater sound travels about four times faster than in air, propagates farther due to water's density, and reaches both ears almost simultaneously, making directional location very difficult.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Panic can be prevented by:

options.c: Properly maintaining your Total Diving System

questions.explanation: Continued training, honest self-assessment of limits, and reliable, well-maintained equipment all reduce stress and the chance of panic. Together they form the prevention strategy taught in SSI courses.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Use of the MySSI Logbook is important because:

options.b: It keeps an accurate record of your personal information and dives
options.c: Provides an overview of visited dive sites
options.d: It is an important information resource for future dives

questions.explanation: The MySSI Logbook records personal information and dive history, gives an overview of visited sites, and is a reference for planning future dives — all of these benefits apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: An object immersed in water will be buoyed upward by a force ______ the weight of the water it displaces.
questions.explanation: Archimedes' Principle states that the buoyant force on an immersed object equals the weight of the fluid the object displaces.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: In an out-of-air emergency, if your buddy is close enough, you should:

options.c: Swim to your buddy, give the out-of-air signal, and share air

questions.explanation: When a buddy is within reach, the safest option is to signal out-of-air and share air using the alternate (octopus) regulator. Independent emergency ascents carry higher risk and are used only when no buddy is available.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: A scuba cylinder for recreational diving should be filled with:

options.a: Pure, filtered compressed air or Nitrox
options.b: A mixture of helium and oxygen
options.c: A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen

questions.explanation: Recreational scuba cylinders are filled with clean, filtered compressed air or with Nitrox (enriched air). Pure oxygen and helium-based mixes require specialized training and equipment.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Signs of a panicked diver are:

options.b: Wide-eyed, fearful look and a fast and erratic breathing pattern
options.c: A diver giving the out of air sign while moving toward you
options.d: A diver heading toward the surface before the scheduled end of the dive

questions.explanation: A panicked diver typically shows wide-open, fearful eyes and a rapid, erratic breathing pattern. Recognizing these signs early lets you intervene before the situation escalates.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: During normal diving activities, divers should never exceed an ascent rate of __ meters per minute.
questions.explanation: SSI recommends a maximum ascent rate of 9 meters (30 feet) per minute to allow nitrogen to off-gas safely and to avoid lung overexpansion injuries.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Overexpansion injuries can be prevented by:

options.b: Never holding your breath while diving
options.c: Learning the correct breathing patterns reinforced in classroom, pool, and open water training
options.d: Dive with a properly maintained high-quality Total Diving System

questions.explanation: Continuous breathing, properly trained breathing patterns, and a reliable Total Diving System all work together to prevent lung overexpansion. All listed measures are valid prevention.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The most efficient breathing pattern for scuba diving is:

options.b: A deep balanced inhalation followed by a long balanced exhalation without any pause between inhaling and exhaling
options.c: Long slow inhalation and faster exhalation

questions.explanation: Deep, balanced, continuous inhalation and exhalation maximize gas exchange, minimize CO2 retention, and prevent breath-holding — the safest and most gas-efficient pattern for scuba.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: If you lose contact with your buddy, you should:

options.b: Exit the water at a predetermined location
options.c: Continue in the direction you were swimming for one minute and then surface
options.d: Search for no more than a minute by turning 360 degrees and looking up and down and then perform a normal ascent to the surface

questions.explanation: The standard buddy-separation procedure is to search for no more than one minute (turning around and looking up/down) and then make a normal ascent to reunite at the surface.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Absolute pressure is defined as:

options.a: The pressure at the surface plus 1 bar
options.b: The total pressure exerted on an object
options.c: The weight of the water that surrounds a diver

questions.explanation: Absolute pressure is the total pressure acting on an object, equal to atmospheric pressure plus the water (gauge) pressure at depth.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The first symptom of a sinus squeeze is usually:

options.a: A sharp pain at the base of the neck
options.b: A dull pain deep within the head
options.c: A sharp pain or wedging sensation above the eyes
options.d: A crushing pain high in the chest

questions.explanation: A sinus squeeze typically presents as a sharp pain or wedging sensation above the eyes (frontal sinuses), caused by pressure imbalance when sinuses cannot equalize.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The greatest relative pressure change in sea water takes place between ____ and ____ meters.
questions.explanation: The pressure doubles between the surface (1 bar) and 10 meters (2 bar) — the largest relative pressure change in any 10-meter increment of the water column.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Divers should avoid contact with all corals because:

options.c: Contact can be harmful to the diver
options.d: Touching corals can damage your equipment

questions.explanation: Even brief contact can kill fragile coral polyps, sting or cut the diver, and abrade equipment. Maintaining good buoyancy and avoiding contact protects everyone involved.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The first thing you should do if you observe signs of panic in a diver at the surface is:

options.c: Swim to the panicked diver and try to drop the diver's weight belt
options.d: Completely fill your buoyancy compensator and instruct the panicked diver to establish positive buoyancy

questions.explanation: Establishing positive buoyancy for yourself and instructing the panicked diver to do the same is the first step. It addresses the immediate life threat (drowning) before any further intervention.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The proper first aid for Arterial Gas embolism, Subcutaneous emphysema, Pneumothorax, and Mediastinal Emphysema is:

options.b: Watch Vital signs, treat for shock and stand by to administer cardio-pulmonary resuscitation (if qualified)
options.d: Seek proper medical aid (a recompression chamber) as quickly as possible

questions.explanation: First aid for any lung overexpansion injury includes 100% oxygen (if qualified), monitoring vitals and treating for shock, and getting the victim to definitive medical care including a recompression chamber.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The Buoyancy Control System components are:

options.a: Buoyancy compensator, exposure suit, and inflation device
options.b: Buoyancy compensator, inflation device, and weight belt or BC weight pockets
options.c: Regulator, inflation device, and weight belt or BC weight pockets

questions.explanation: The SSI Buoyancy Control System consists of the BC, the inflation device (low-pressure inflator), and the weight system (weight belt or integrated weight pockets).
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The term No-decompression Limit is defined as:

options.a: The bottom time recorded during a dive
options.b: The maximum depth recorded during the dive
options.c: The maximum allowed time at depth without having to perform a mandatory decompression stop during the ascent
options.d: The maximum surface interval allowed during a day's diving

questions.explanation: The No-Decompression Limit (NDL) is the maximum time a diver may stay at a given depth and still ascend directly to the surface without needing a mandatory decompression stop.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The SSI Responsible Diver Code states that you should:

options.a: Dive within the limits of your training and ability
options.c: Accept responsibility for your own well-being on every dive
options.d: Be environmentally responsible on every dive

questions.explanation: The SSI Responsible Diver Code includes diving within training and ability, accepting personal responsibility, and being environmentally responsible — all of these apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: If you experience pain in the ear during descent:

options.a: Continue the descent while gently exhaling into the mask
options.c: Place a finger in the ear canal and gently massage the ear until the pain ceases
options.d: Stop the descent and ascend until the pain stops

questions.explanation: If equalization fails and pain occurs, stop descending and ascend slightly until the pain stops, then try to equalize again. Forcing the descent can cause an ear barotrauma.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Causes of panic include:

options.b: Using equipment that is unfamiliar or does not fit well
options.d: Diving outside a diver's comfort and ability

questions.explanation: Panic can be triggered by adverse environmental conditions, unfamiliar or ill-fitting equipment, or exceeding personal comfort and ability — all of these are recognized causes.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Underwater, the bending of light rays causes objects to appear to be:

options.d: At an angle consistent with the bend of the light

questions.explanation: Refraction at the air–water interface of the mask makes objects appear about 25% closer (and 33% larger) than they actually are.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: In air at 1 bar, the partial pressure of nitrogen is___, oxygen is_____.
questions.explanation: Air is approximately 79% nitrogen and 21% oxygen, so at 1 bar total pressure the partial pressures are 0.79 bar N2 and 0.21 bar O2 (Dalton's Law).
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: To use a compass to navigate to a sighted object:

options.a: Point the magnetic needle toward the object and swim
options.b: Point the lubber line toward the object and swim
options.c: Point the compass toward the object, rotate the bezel until the witness marks are over the magnetic needle, and follow the lubber line
options.d: Point the witness marks toward the object and swim

questions.explanation: Aim the compass at the target with the lubber line, rotate the bezel so the witness (index) marks frame the magnetic needle, then swim while keeping the needle inside the marks and following the lubber line.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Sinus squeeze can be prevented by:

options.a: Tilting the divers head back and forth

questions.explanation: Congestion from a cold blocks sinus passages, preventing equalization. The most reliable prevention is simply not diving when congested.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: A good diving buddy:

options.a: Is able to help in all phases of diving and dive planning
options.b: Is as familiar with their buddy's diving equipment as they are with their own

questions.explanation: A good buddy assists in planning and execution, knows their partner's equipment, and adds enjoyment to the dive — all qualities apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The purpose of a BC is to:

options.d: Maintain neutral buoyancy during the dive

questions.explanation: A buoyancy compensator controls ascent/descent rates, provides positive surface flotation, and lets the diver maintain neutral buoyancy at depth — all listed purposes apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Residual nitrogen is defined as:

options.a: The amount of excessive nitrogen dissolved in our blood stream and tissues at the beginning of the initial ascent to the surface
options.c: The amount of excessive nitrogen dissolved in our blood stream and tissues after a dive
options.d: The amount of nitrogen dissolved in our blood stream and tissues at the beginning of the first dive of the day

questions.explanation: Residual nitrogen is the excess (above-equilibrium) nitrogen still dissolved in blood and tissues after a dive. It must be accounted for when planning a repetitive dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: When diving in Marine Protected Areas like Marine Parks, divers should:

options.a: Respect and follow the local Marine Park regulations
options.b: Follow the same diving practices as outside the parks
options.c: Only collect marine life that is not protected by federal law

questions.explanation: Each Marine Protected Area has its own regulations designed to protect that ecosystem. Divers must learn and respect those local rules whenever they dive there.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: A dive computer keeps track of a diver's nitrogen absorption levels so that the diver can:

options.d: Stay within the no-decompression limits on each dive

questions.explanation: Dive computers continuously calculate tissue nitrogen loading and display remaining no-decompression time, helping the diver stay within NDL on every dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Dive computers are essential for planning and executing dives because they:

options.d: Help divers stay within the no-decompression limits
