questions.explanation: The dive reflex is a set of physiological responses (bradycardia, peripheral vasoconstriction, blood shift) triggered by breath-holding, especially with face/cold-water immersion.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What factors can increase the risk of immersion pulmonary edema?

options.c: Diving in cold water and inspiring against a negative pressure

questions.explanation: IPE risk is multifactorial: cardiac issues, cold water, breathing against negative pressure, and high exertion/stress all contribute.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: A physiological condition called a squeeze occurs when:

options.a: The pressure on the outside of an closed air space is greater than the internal pressure
options.b: The pressure on the inside of an air space is greater than the external pressure
options.c: The pressure on the inside of an air space is increasing to equalize with the external pressure
options.d: None of the answers are correct

questions.explanation: A squeeze (barotrauma of descent) occurs when ambient pressure outside an air-filled space exceeds the pressure inside it, deforming or injuring the surrounding tissues.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is a reverse block?

options.a: A squeeze in reverse that happens when compressing gas becomes trapped on descent
options.b: A squeeze in reverse that happens when expanding gas becomes trapped on ascent
options.c: A blockage in the middle ear
options.d: A arterial blockage caused by gas bubbles

questions.explanation: A reverse block is barotrauma of ascent: expanding gas is trapped in an air space (sinus, ear, lung) and cannot escape, causing pain or injury.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are parts of the ear?

options.a: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, ossicles, Eustachian tube, and bronchioles
options.b: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, inner ear, Eustachian tube, and maxillary sinuses
options.c: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, ossicles, inner ear, round and oval windows, and Eustachian tube
options.d: The earlobe, middle ear, inner ear, and Valsalva

questions.explanation: The ear comprises outer ear, eardrum, middle ear with ossicles, inner ear, the round and oval windows, and the Eustachian tube.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are signs and symptoms of sinus barotrauma?

options.a: Pain behind the eyes, teeth, or cheekbones

questions.explanation: Sinus barotrauma can present as facial pain (eyes, teeth, cheeks), nasal congestion or bleeding, and headache — all listed symptoms apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What preventative measures can help prevent barotrauma?

options.a: Equalize the ears, sinuses and lungs frequently

questions.explanation: Frequent equalization, slow controlled descents/ascents, and never breath-holding all reduce barotrauma risk — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: A diver experiencing pain in their ears or sinuses while descending should:

options.a: Return to the surface and re-initiate the descent
options.c: Ascend until the pressure is relieved and then attempt equalization
options.d: Stop and wait until the discomfort subsides

questions.explanation: If equalization fails on descent, ascend slightly until the pressure is relieved, then re-attempt equalization gently — never force.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: The term "decompression illness" covers which diving-related disorders?

options.a: Type I and Type II decompression sickness and hyperoxia
options.b: Decompression sickness, lung overexpansion, and arterial gas embolism

questions.explanation: Decompression illness (DCI) is the umbrella term covering decompression sickness, lung overexpansion injuries, and arterial gas embolism.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which predisposing factors can increase the risk of decompression sickness?

options.a: A high fitness level, being under the age of 40, and exposure to warm water
options.b: A low fitness level, being over the age of 50, and exposure to cold water
options.c: Being well-rested, having a healthy diet, and being an experienced diver
options.d: Using loose-fitting equipment, being dehydrated, and diving in clear water

questions.explanation: Low fitness, advanced age, and cold water exposure are recognized predisposing factors that increase DCS risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What areas of the body are typically affected by Type II decompression sickness?
questions.explanation: Type II DCS is the serious form and can affect the central nervous system, inner ear, and cardiorespiratory system — all listed areas apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: When can signs of decompression sickness appear?
questions.explanation: DCS symptoms can appear immediately, within minutes, or be delayed for many hours after surfacing — all listed time frames are possible.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is an arterial gas embolism?

options.b: The blockage of a vein by bubbles
options.c: The blockage of an arterial blood vessel by bubbles
options.d: Blue lips caused by low oxygen

questions.explanation: An arterial gas embolism (AGE) is the obstruction of an arterial blood vessel by gas bubbles, often resulting from pulmonary barotrauma.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How might gas bubbles enter arterial blood vessels?

options.a: Through a cut in the diver's skin
options.d: Pulmonary barotrauma or shunting through a patent foramen ovale

questions.explanation: Bubbles enter arterial circulation primarily via pulmonary barotrauma (lung overexpansion) or by right-to-left shunt through a patent foramen ovale (PFO).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the established treatment for decompression illness?

options.c: No treatment, the diver must wait to off-gas

questions.explanation: The definitive treatment for DCI is recompression in a hyperbaric chamber breathing oxygen, which shrinks bubbles and improves tissue oxygenation.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the immediate care in a suspected decompression illness emergency?

options.d: Monitor vital signs and be ready to perform CPR

questions.explanation: First aid for DCI includes administering 100% oxygen, monitoring vitals, being ready to perform CPR, and seeking emergency medical assistance — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Hypoxia is a condition resulting from:

options.b: Lack of oxygen in the body
options.c: Excess oxygen in the body tissues
options.d: Elevated carbon dioxide levels in the blood

questions.explanation: Hypoxia is an inadequate supply of oxygen to body tissues, resulting in impaired function and potentially loss of consciousness.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How does hyperventilation possibly cause hypoxia?

options.a: It massively increases the amount of oxygen a diver inhales
options.b: It causes an imbalance between oxygen intake and carbon dioxide levels
options.d: It decreases blood carbon monoxide levels

questions.explanation: Hyperventilation lowers CO2 without significantly increasing O2; the reduced CO2 delays the urge to breathe, allowing oxygen to drop dangerously low during a breath-hold (shallow water blackout).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are two potential hazards of breathing higher concentrations of oxygen?

options.c: Hemoglobin dysfunction and carbon monoxide poisoning
options.d: Central nervous system oxygen toxicity and pulmonary oxygen toxicity

questions.explanation: Elevated oxygen exposure can cause acute CNS oxygen toxicity (convulsions) at high partial pressures and pulmonary oxygen toxicity (lung irritation) with prolonged exposure.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: At what partial pressure of oxygen can hypoxia occur?
questions.explanation: Hypoxia generally occurs when the partial pressure of oxygen drops below about 0.16 ata, insufficient to oxygenate the tissues.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of central nervous system oxygen toxicity include:

options.a: Coughing and a burning sensation in the lungs

questions.explanation: CNS oxygen toxicity is recognized by the VENTID-C symptoms; the classic and most dangerous sign is convulsions/twitching.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is inert gas narcosis?

options.a: Physical and mental disturbance caused by breathing gas at a reduced pressure
options.b: Physical and mental disturbance caused by breathing gas at an elevated pressure
options.c: A disturbance caused by divers drinking too many martinis

questions.explanation: Inert gas narcosis is a reversible alteration in mental and physical performance caused by breathing inert gases (especially nitrogen) at elevated partial pressures.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of inert gas narcosis include:
questions.explanation: Narcosis can produce euphoria, impaired memory, and slowed response times — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Hypercapnia can be prevented:

options.a: By breathing in a controlled manner

questions.explanation: Preventing CO2 build-up requires controlled breathing, limiting workload, and well-maintained equipment with low breathing resistance — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is hypothermia?

options.a: A medical condition where the body produces or absorbs more heat than it can dissipate.
options.b: A medical condition caused by freezing of the skin and underlying tissues.
options.c: A medical condition where the body produces too much carbon dioxide.
options.d: A medical condition where the body loses heat faster than it can produce heat.

questions.explanation: Hypothermia is the abnormal lowering of core body temperature when heat loss outpaces heat production.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of severe hypothermia can include:

options.a: Absence of shivering, decreased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness
options.b: Uncontrollable shivering, decreased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness
options.d: Uncontrollable shivering, increased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness

questions.explanation: In severe hypothermia, shivering stops as the body's thermoregulation fails, heart and breathing rates slow, and the diver may become unconscious.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Why does hypothermia increase the risk of decompression sickness?

options.a: The dilation of blood vessels reduces circulation
options.b: The dilation of blood vessels increases heat absorption
options.c: The constriction of blood vessels increases blood flow
options.d: The constriction of blood vessels reduces circulation

questions.explanation: Cold causes peripheral vasoconstriction, reducing blood flow to tissues; that slows off-gassing during ascent, increasing DCS risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How might divers be at risk of hyperthermia?

options.a: They overheat by wearing thermal insulation in warm environments

questions.explanation: Hyperthermia risk arises from over-insulation in warm environments, overexertion, and dehydration — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What actions can divers take to reduce the risks of hyperthermia?

options.b: Don wetsuits just before the dive

questions.explanation: Hydrating, donning the suit just before entry, and minimizing exertion all reduce hyperthermia risk — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Why must o-rings be properly maintained and replaced if necessary?

options.a: They help the equipment move through the water
options.b: They provide additional buoyancy to the equipment
options.c: They regulate the speed breathing gas
options.d: They prevent water from entering equipment and keep the internal parts dry

questions.explanation: O-rings seal mating surfaces against water entry; if degraded, water can enter and damage the equipment internals.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How often should equipment be serviced?

options.b: At least annually and according to manufacturer instructions
options.c: Once a week and according to manufacturer instructions

questions.explanation: Dive equipment should be serviced at least annually and per the manufacturer's instructions to ensure safety and reliability.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following statements about steel cylinders is true?

options.a: They are made of denser and stronger material than aluminum
options.b: They are generally more negatively or neutrally buoyant compared to aluminum cylinders
options.d: They may be preferred when thicker wetsuits increase buoyancy

questions.explanation: Steel cylinders are denser and stronger than aluminum, are typically more negative/neutral in water, and are preferred when extra ballast is desirable — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are the two different types of valve connections?
questions.explanation: Cylinder valves use either yoke (A-clamp/INT) or DIN (threaded) connections to mate with the regulator first stage.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true about cylinder care and maintenance?

options.a: Cylinders should be inspected frequently for signs of damage or corrosion
options.c: Cylinders should be kept with a minimum gas pressure of twenty to fifty bar
options.d: Valves used with aluminum cylinders must be periodically lubricated

questions.explanation: Frequent inspection, maintaining a minimum residual pressure (20-50 bar), and periodic valve servicing are all part of correct cylinder care — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: During transport, cylinders should be:
questions.explanation: When transporting cylinders the key requirement is that they are properly secured so they cannot roll, fall, or impact other items.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the basic purpose of the first and second stages?

options.a: The first stage reduces high-pressure cylinder gas to intermediate pressure
options.b: The second stage reduces intermediate-pressure gas to ambient pressure
options.c: First- and second-stage valves open and close according to demand

questions.explanation: The first stage drops cylinder pressure to intermediate, the second stage drops intermediate to ambient on demand — all statements apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Regulator icing is caused by the:
questions.explanation: Regulator icing is caused by the adiabatic cooling that occurs when breathing gas rapidly expands across the first/second stage in cold water.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Oxygen-compatible equipment is equipment that is:

options.a: Needs adapting for use with nitrox
options.d: Manufactured ready for use with nitrox

questions.explanation: Oxygen-compatible equipment is built and prepared from the manufacturer to safely handle elevated O2 mixtures (nitrox) without modification.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are the main inflation mechanisms on a buoyancy compensator?

options.a: The power inflator and power deflator
options.b: The power inflator and oral inflator
options.c: The power inflator and dump valves
options.d: The oral inflator and exhaust button

questions.explanation: A BC is inflated either via the power inflator (using cylinder gas) or via the oral inflator (using exhaled breath).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are trim weights?

options.a: Adjustable weights that help the diver achieve proper trim
options.c: Weights used only at the surface to assist with flotation

questions.explanation: Trim weights are small, repositionable weights used to fine-tune body attitude (horizontal trim) underwater.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Neoprene provides insulation because it contains:
questions.explanation: Neoprene's insulating properties come from millions of closed gas-filled cells in the foam that resist heat transfer.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: When wetsuits get thicker, buoyancy:
questions.explanation: Thicker neoprene contains more gas cells and more displaced water volume, so buoyancy increases with wetsuit thickness.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following statements about wetsuit compression is true?

options.a: It decreases positive buoyancy at depth

questions.explanation: At depth, neoprene compresses; this reduces both insulation and the suit's buoyancy — all statements are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: A submersible pressure gauge is connected to the:
questions.explanation: An SPG is fed cylinder pressure directly via the first-stage high-pressure (HP) port to display tank pressure.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How does a gas-integrated dive computer help divers manage their gas?

options.a: By automatically adjusting the diver's breathing rate
options.b: By monitoring and displaying the remaining gas supply in real-time
options.d: By storing historical data about the diver's gas consumption

questions.explanation: Gas-integrated computers read cylinder pressure (wirelessly or hose-fed) and display remaining gas, often calculating remaining time based on current consumption.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is an advantage of using a watch-style computer as a dive computer?

options.a: They do not require additional hoses or consoles
options.b: They are designed to be worn on the wrist like a regular watch

questions.explanation: Watch-style computers need no hose/console, are wrist-worn for everyday use, and are light and streamlined — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following statements about nitrox dive computers is true?

options.b: Most standard dive computers are nitrox-capable
options.c: Nitrox computers can be used for air dives
options.d: Nitrox computers calculate data based on increased oxygen levels

questions.explanation: Modern computers are typically nitrox-capable, can be used for air dives, and adjust calculations for the chosen oxygen percentage — all statements apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Styles of dive flag include:

options.a: The Alpha flag and national flag
options.b: The Alpha flag and recreational diver flag
options.c: The recreational diver flag and boat flag
options.d: The recreational diver flag and national flag

questions.explanation: Two common dive flags are the international Alpha flag (vessel restricted) and the recreational diver flag (red with white diagonal stripe).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: When does a diver deploy a delayed surface marker buoy?

options.b: To mark the descent at the beginning of the dive
options.d: To mark the ascent or at the end of the dive

questions.explanation: A DSMB is deployed during ascent or at the end of the dive to mark the diver's position to surface support.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which features distinguish a reel from a spool?

options.a: A spool is preferred for longer deployments
options.b: A reel has a handle, a brake or locking mechanism, and a winding knob
options.d: A reel is always smaller and lighter

questions.explanation: A reel is distinguished by its handle, locking/brake mechanism, and winding knob; a spool is a simple line holder with no moving parts.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Why should divers carry a cutting device?

options.a: To carve navigational markers into underwater rocks
options.b: To signal other divers through metal clanging
options.c: To hunt underwater creatures more effectively
options.d: To respond to underwater entanglements hazards

questions.explanation: A cutting tool is essential for freeing the diver or buddy from line, net or other entanglement hazards.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Why should divers carry multiple signaling devices?

options.a: Carrying more devices lessens the chance of losing any single one
options.b: To be prepared for different emergency situations
options.c: Device diversity helps divers maintain buoyancy control
options.d: Having multiple devices encourages faster underwater navigation

questions.explanation: Different signaling devices (audible, visual, day/night, surface/underwater) cover different emergency scenarios and conditions.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What happens when a wave reaches the surf zone?

options.a: The wave speeds up and continues moving offshore
options.b: The wave completely stops moving forward
options.c: The wave becomes a tidal bore and moves inland
options.d: The wave slows down, increases in height, and breaks

questions.explanation: As a wave enters shallow water its base slows, the wave shortens and grows in height until it becomes unstable and breaks.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the primary function of seagrass beds in marine ecosystems?

options.a: They generate waves and strong currents
options.b: They prevent all sediment from settling on the seafloor
options.c: They stop seahorses being consumed by predators
options.d: They provide food and habitat for various marine species

questions.explanation: Seagrass beds are critical nursery habitat and food source for many marine species and stabilize sediments.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Salinity increases in regions affected by:
questions.explanation: Where evaporation exceeds precipitation (and freshwater inputs), water leaves but salt stays — increasing salinity.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: A thermocline is a water layer where:
questions.explanation: A thermocline is a layer in a body of water where temperature changes more rapidly with depth than in the layers above or below.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Plants and phytoplankton use carbon dioxide to:

options.a: Produce energy and release oxygen during photosynthesis

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis converts CO2 and water (using sunlight) into energy/sugars and releases oxygen as a byproduct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between wind and wave formation?

options.a: Wind creates waves by increasing ocean salinity
options.b: Wind transfers energy to the water's surface, generating waves
options.c: Wind has little effect on ocean surface conditions
options.d: Wind causes water to cool, preventing waves from forming

questions.explanation: Surface waves are created by wind transferring kinetic energy to the water surface; stronger/longer winds produce larger waves.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Understanding the period of a wave helps divers:

options.d: Time their movements and navigate safely

questions.explanation: Knowing wave period lets divers time entries, exits, and movements with the wave cycle for safer navigation in surf and surge.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How can waves affect diving conditions?

options.b: Larges waves in the surf zone can be hazardous
options.c: Choppy seas and rough conditions can make exits and entries difficult
options.d: Wave action can reduce underwater visibility

questions.explanation: Waves can create surf-zone hazards, complicate entries/exits, and reduce visibility by stirring up sediment — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What should divers do if they are caught in a strong current?

options.b: Swim parallel/at an angle to the shore or drift with the current and wait to be picked up
options.c: Fight the current for as long as they can
options.d: Add more weight so they can stay in one place

questions.explanation: Don't fight a strong current; swim across it (parallel/at an angle) toward shore, or drift and signal for pickup.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is plankton?

options.d: A diverse group of organisms that cannot swim effectively and drift with ocean currents

questions.explanation: Plankton is a diverse assemblage of organisms (bacteria, algae, protists, animals) that drift with currents because they cannot swim effectively against them.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are the three main groups of fishes?

options.a: Bony fishes, cartilaginous fishes, and orca
options.b: Cartilaginous fishes, bony fishes, and carnivorous fishes
options.c: Bony fishes, cartilaginous fishes, and jawless fishes

questions.explanation: The three main groups are bony fishes (Osteichthyes), cartilaginous fishes (Chondrichthyes), and jawless fishes (Agnatha).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How can divers minimize negative interactions with marine life?

options.a: Move cautiously, avoid sudden movements, maintain buoyancy, and avoid covered areas
options.b: Observe, but do not touch or bait animals
options.d: Wear appropriate exposure suits and follow local regulations

questions.explanation: Cautious movement, no touching/baiting, appropriate exposure protection, and following local regulations all reduce negative interactions — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: A thermocline is a water layer where:
questions.explanation: Thermocline = layer where temperature changes rapidly with depth (halocline = salinity).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are components of the blood?

options.c: Water, enzymes, hormones, and clotting factors

questions.explanation: Blood contains red and white cells, platelets, plus plasma (water, enzymes, hormones, clotting factors) - all components.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: A diver has a mass of 87 kilograms when neutrally buoyant in seawater. To be neutrally buoyant in fresh water, they must:
questions.explanation: Fresh water is less dense than seawater, so it provides less buoyant support; the diver must remove weight.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of inert gas narcosis include:
questions.explanation: Inert gas narcosis causes euphoria, impaired memory, and slowed responses - all are symptoms.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: When can signs of decompression sickness appear?
questions.explanation: DCS signs can appear immediately on surfacing, within 15 minutes, or up to 12+ hours later - all timeframes possible.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Salinity increases in regions affected by:
questions.explanation: Low rainfall plus high evaporation concentrates dissolved salts, increasing salinity.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Regulator icing is caused by the:
questions.explanation: Rapid expansion of breathing gas at the first/second stage cools moisture and can freeze the regulator.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of central nervous system oxygen toxicity include:

options.a: Coughing and a burning sensation in the lungs

questions.explanation: CNS oxygen toxicity classic signs include convulsions and twitching (the 'C' in ConVENTID).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Where does gas exchange take place in the lungs?

options.a: Through pulmonary capillaries in the alveoli
options.b: Through pulmonary capillaries in the trachea
options.c: Through arteries in the vena cava

questions.explanation: Gas exchange occurs across pulmonary capillaries surrounding the alveoli.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is a function of the cardiovascular system?

options.a: To transport carbon dioxide from the lungs to the body, and transport oxygen back to the lungs
options.c: To transport oxygen from the lungs to the body, and transport carbon dioxide back to the lungs

questions.explanation: The cardiovascular system transports oxygen from lungs to body tissues and CO2 back to lungs for exhalation.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Neoprene provides insulation because it contains:
questions.explanation: Neoprene insulates because of closed gas-filled cells; this is also why it compresses with depth.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: When is a gas supersaturated?

options.a: When there is less gas in solution than the substance can hold at equilibrium
options.b: When the pressure of the gases in and out of solution become equal
options.c: When the pressure of the gas in solution exceeds the pressure of the gas out of solution
options.d: When it is full of gas

questions.explanation: A gas is supersaturated when its dissolved-pressure exceeds the surrounding (out-of-solution) pressure.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: When does a diver deploy a delayed surface marker buoy?

options.a: To mark the ascent or at the end of the dive
options.c: To mark the descent at the beginning of the dive

questions.explanation: A delayed SMB is deployed at the start of the ascent (or end of dive) to mark position for the boat.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are two potential hazards of breathing higher concentrations of oxygen?

options.c: Central nervous system oxygen toxicity and pulmonary oxygen toxicity
options.d: Hemoglobin dysfunction and carbon monoxide poisoning

questions.explanation: Breathing high pO2 risks both CNS and pulmonary oxygen toxicity.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: The term “decompression illness" covers which diving- related disorders?

options.a: Type I and Type II decompression sickness and hyperoxia
options.d: Decompression sickness, lung overexpansion, and arterial gas embolism

questions.explanation: Decompression illness umbrella covers DCS, lung overexpansion injuries, and arterial gas embolism.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Why does hypothermia increase the risk of decompression sickness?

options.a: The dilation of blood vessels reduces circulation
options.b: The dilation of blood vessels increases heat absorption
options.c: The constriction of blood vessels reduces circulation
options.d: The constriction of blood vessels increases blood flow

questions.explanation: Hypothermia constricts peripheral blood vessels, reducing circulation and impairing inert-gas elimination.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is plankton?

options.a: A diverse group of organisms that cannot swim effectively and drift with ocean currents

questions.explanation: Plankton is a diverse group of drifting organisms that cannot swim effectively against currents.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are the main inflation mechanisms on a buoyancy compensator?

options.a: The power inflator and oral inflator
options.b: The power inflator and power deflator
options.c: The power inflator and dump valves
options.d: The oral inflator and exhaust button

questions.explanation: A BC is inflated via the power inflator and the oral inflator (backup).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is a diver’s SAC rate if they dive for 10 minutes at a depth of 10 meters, and consume 50 bar from a 10-liter cylinder?
questions.explanation: SAC: 50 bar x 10 L = 500 L over 10 min at 2 ATA = 25 L/min surface equivalent.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How do colors change as a diver descends?

options.b: Red, orange and yellow lose their color quite shallow
options.c: On a deep dive in clear water divers will see blue, indigo, and violet

questions.explanation: Colors fade rapidly with depth - red/orange/yellow first, then on deep clear dives blue/indigo/violet remain.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following statements about steel cylinders is true?

options.a: They are made of denser and stronger material than aluminum
options.b: They are generally more negatively or neutrally buoyant compared to aluminum cylinders
options.d: They may be preferred when thicker wetsuits increase buoyancy

questions.explanation: Steel is denser/stronger than aluminum, more negatively/neutrally buoyant, and preferred with thick wetsuits - all true.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Gradient factors adjust the level of conservatism by:

options.a: Increasing the maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann’s original values
options.b: Adjusting the temperature gradient in water
options.c: Lowering the maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann’s original values
options.d: Adjusting the diver’s inert gas absorption

questions.explanation: Gradient factors lower the maximum tolerated supersaturation vs. Buhlmann original M-values to add conservatism.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between wind and wave formation?

options.a: Wind creates waves by increasing ocean salinity
options.b: Wind has little effect on ocean surface conditions
options.c: Wind transfers energy to the water’s surface, generating waves
options.d: Wind causes water to cool, preventing waves from forming

questions.explanation: Wind transfers energy to the water surface, generating waves.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: During transport, cylinders should be:
questions.explanation: Cylinders being transported should be properly secured to prevent movement and damage.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of severe hypothermia can include:

options.a: Uncontrollable shivering, decreased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness
options.b: Absence of shivering, decreased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness
options.d: Uncontrollable shivering, increased heart and breathing rate, and unconsciousness

questions.explanation: Severe hypothermia: shivering stops, heart and breathing rate decrease, and unconsciousness occurs.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the definition of “half-time" ?

options.a: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to absorb half the total amount of an inert gas that it would contain if it were completely saturated at a specific depth or partial pressure
options.b: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to absorb twice the total amount of an inert gas that it would contain if it were completely saturated at a specific depth or partial pressure
options.c: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to eliminate the nitrogen it contains
options.d: The number of minutes required for a diver complete half their dive

questions.explanation: Half-time = minutes for a tissue to absorb half of the inert gas it would hold at full saturation for that pressure.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the basic purpose of the first and second stages?

options.a: The first stage reduces high- pressure cylinder gas to intermediate pressure
options.b: The second stage reduces intermediate-pressure gas to ambient pressure
options.d: First- and second-stage valves open and close according to demand

questions.explanation: First stage: HP -> IP. Second stage: IP -> ambient on demand. Valves open/close per demand. All correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Multi-level diving complicates decompression theory because:

options.a: The diver is exposed to reduced atmospheric pressure
options.b: The drop in pressure can release nitrogen from solution
options.c: The diver’s tissues continually saturate with nitrogen throughout the dive
options.d: The diver’s tissues saturate and desaturate throughout the dive

questions.explanation: In multilevel dives, tissues both saturate (deeper) and desaturate (shallower) throughout the dive, complicating modeling.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following statements about wetsuit compression is true?

options.a: It decreases positive buoyancy at depth

questions.explanation: With depth, neoprene compresses, insulation drops, and positive buoyancy decreases - all true.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the no- decompression limit based on?

options.a: Dive depth, duration, surface interval, and tissue nitrogen absorption
options.d: The number of required decompression stops

questions.explanation: NDL is computed from depth, duration, surface interval, and the resulting tissue nitrogen loading.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following statements about evaporative heat loss is true?

options.a: When water evaporates it takes heat energy away from the body
options.b: Divers can experience evaporative heat loss when water evaporates from their skin
options.c: Dry breathing gas can cause evaporative heat loss as mucus linings dry out in the lungs

questions.explanation: Evaporation removes heat from skin and from drying mucus in the lungs - all answers describe true mechanisms.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What does it mean if a body tissue is poorly-perfused?

options.b: It has a good blood supply

questions.explanation: Poorly-perfused tissues have poor blood supply and therefore eliminate (and absorb) inert gas slowly.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What preventative measures can help prevent barotrauma?

options.a: Equalize the ears, sinuses and lungs frequently

questions.explanation: Equalize early/often, avoid rapid pressure changes, and never hold the breath - all help prevent barotrauma.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are the two different types of valve connections?
questions.explanation: The two valve connection standards are yoke (international/A-clamp) and DIN.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: When wetsuits get thicker, buoyancy:
questions.explanation: Thicker wetsuits contain more closed gas cells, which increases buoyancy (and requires more lead).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the mammalian dive reflex?

options.a: A series of psychological changes that result from seeing mammals in the water
options.b: A series of physiological changes that result from breath-holds, especially during immersion
options.c: A reflex that makes humans want to dive
options.d: A series of physiological changes that result from tachypnea during a dive

questions.explanation: The mammalian dive reflex is a set of physiological changes (bradycardia, vasoconstriction) triggered by breath-hold and facial immersion.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Understanding how gas solubility relates to pressure helps divers understand:

options.a: How far they can push their limits
options.b: How gases expand and compress in equipment
options.c: How much gas they can safely breathe on a dive
options.d: How gases are absorbed into body tissues and the risks associated with decompression illness

questions.explanation: Gas-solubility/pressure relationships explain inert-gas absorption in tissues and the resulting decompression risks.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the established treatment for decompression illness?

options.c: No treatment, the diver must wait to off-gas

questions.explanation: Definitive DCI treatment is recompression in a hyperbaric chamber with oxygen.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is hypothermia?

options.a: A medical condition where the body loses heat faster than it can produce heat.
options.b: A medical condition where the body produces or absorbs more heat than it can dissipate.
options.c: A medical condition caused by freezing of the skin and underlying tissues.
options.d: A medical condition where the body produces too much carbon dioxide.

questions.explanation: Hypothermia = body loses heat faster than it can produce it.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true about cylinder care and maintenance?

options.a: Cylinders should be inspected frequently for signs of damage or corrosion
options.c: Cylinders should be kept with a minimum gas pressure of twenty to fifty bar
options.d: Valves used with aluminum cylinders must be periodically lubricated

questions.explanation: Inspect for damage/corrosion, keep 20-50 bar minimum residual pressure, lubricate aluminum-cylinder valves periodically - all true.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What happens when a wave reaches the surf zone?

options.a: The wave speeds up and continues moving offshore
options.b: The wave completely stops moving forward
options.c: The wave slows down, increases in height, and breaks
options.d: The wave becomes a tidal bore and moves inland

questions.explanation: In the surf zone the wave slows, builds height, then breaks.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Oxygen-compatible equipment is equipment that is:

options.a: Needs adapting for use with nitrox
options.d: Manufactured ready for use with nitrox

questions.explanation: Oxygen-compatible equipment is manufactured ready for use with nitrox (cleaned/built for elevated O2).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How might gas bubbles enter arterial blood vessels?

options.a: Through a cut in the diver’s skin
options.b: Pulmonary barotrauma or shunting through a patent foramen ovale

questions.explanation: Arterial bubbles enter via pulmonary barotrauma or by shunting through a patent foramen ovale (PFO).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is an advantage of using a watch-style computer as a dive computer?

options.a: They do not require additional hoses or consoles
options.b: They are designed to be worn on the wrist like a regular watch

questions.explanation: Watch-style computers are wrist-worn, lightweight, streamlined, and need no extra hose/console - all advantages.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is a pressure gradient?

options.a: The force resulting from equal pressures
options.b: The force resulting from a difference in pressure
options.c: The force of a substance of higher pressure
options.d: The difference in concentrations within a substance or across two areas

questions.explanation: A pressure gradient is the force resulting from a difference in pressure between two regions.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Styles of dive flag include:

options.a: The Alpha flag and national flag
options.b: The Alpha flag and recreational diver flag
options.c: The recreational diver flag and boat flag
options.d: The recreational diver flag and national flag

questions.explanation: Recreational diver flag (red with white diagonal stripe) and Alpha flag (international maritime) are the two dive-flag styles.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What actions can divers take to reduce the risks of hyperthermia?

options.c: Don wetsuits just before the dive

questions.explanation: Hydrate, don wetsuits just before entry, and minimize exertion - all reduce hyperthermia risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Why should divers carry multiple signaling devices?

options.a: To be prepared for different emergency situations
options.b: Carrying more devices lessens the chance of losing any single one
options.c: Device diversity helps divers maintain buoyancy control
options.d: Having multiple devices encourages faster underwater navigation

questions.explanation: Multiple signaling devices cover varied emergencies and provide redundancy if one is lost.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are signs and symptoms of sinus barotrauma?

options.a: Pain behind the eyes, teeth, or cheekbones

questions.explanation: Sinus barotrauma symptoms include pain behind eyes/teeth/cheekbones, congestion or bleeding, and headache - all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Archimedes’ Principle states that:

options.a: The sum of partial pressures in a gas mixture is equal to the total gas pressure
options.b: The amount of a gas dissolved in a liquid is proportional to its partial pressure
options.c: An object placed in a fluid will be depressed by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces
options.d: An object placed in a fluid will be buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces

questions.explanation: Archimedes: an object in a fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the displaced fluid.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How does an increase in gas pressure influence tissue saturation?
questions.explanation: Higher ambient gas pressure raises the partial pressure gradient driving inert gas into tissues, increasing saturation.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following statements about carbon dioxide is true?

options.b: It is a natural byproduct of the body's metabolism
options.c: It should be eliminated from breathing mixtures
options.d: High concentrations will disrupt normal bodily functions

questions.explanation: CO2 is a metabolic byproduct, must be eliminated from breathing gas, and high levels disrupt normal function - all true.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: The "apparent mass" (the buoyancy) of an object can be calculated:

options.a: By adding the weight of the displaced water to the mass of the immersed object
options.b: By subtracting the mass of the displaced water from the mass of the immersed object
options.c: By testing the mass of the object using scales in water
options.d: By weighing it down and seeing if it will float

questions.explanation: Apparent mass = mass of object minus mass of displaced water (Archimedes).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are parts of the ear?

options.a: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, ossicles, Eustachian tube, and bronchioles
options.b: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, ossicles, inner ear, round and oval windows, and Eustachian tube
options.c: The outer ear, eardrum, middle ear, inner ear, Eustachian tube, and maxillary sinuses
options.d: The earlobe, middle ear, inner ear, and Valsalva

questions.explanation: Ear anatomy: outer ear, eardrum, middle ear with ossicles, inner ear with round/oval windows, and the Eustachian tube.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What should divers do if they are caught in a strong current?

options.b: Fight the current for as long as they can
options.c: Add more weight so they can stay in one place
options.d: Swim parallel/at an angle to the shore or drift with the current and wait to be picked up

questions.explanation: Don't fight a current; swim parallel/at an angle to shore or drift and signal for pickup.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: The absolute pressure at a depth of fifty meters:
questions.explanation: Absolute pressure = 1 bar (surface) + 5 bar (50 m water) = 6.0 bar.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which blood vessels are used for gas exchange in the body?
questions.explanation: Capillaries are the thin-walled vessels where gas, nutrient, and waste exchange occurs at the tissues and lungs.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: A physiological condition called a squeeze occurs when:

options.a: The pressure on the inside of an air space is greater than the external pressure
options.b: The pressure on the outside of an closed air space is greater than the internal pressure
options.c: The pressure on the inside of an air space is increasing to equalize with the external pressure
options.d: None of the answers are correct

questions.explanation: A squeeze (descent barotrauma) occurs when external pressure exceeds internal pressure of a closed air space.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How often should equipment be serviced?

options.b: At least annually and according to manufacturer instructions
options.c: Once a week and according to manufacturer instructions

questions.explanation: Service equipment at least annually and follow the manufacturer's specific instructions/intervals.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Hypoxia is a condition resulting from:

options.b: Excess oxygen in the body tissues
options.c: Lack of oxygen in the body
options.d: Elevated carbon dioxide levels in the blood

questions.explanation: Hypoxia = insufficient oxygen reaching the body tissues.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is inert gas narcosis?

options.a: Physical and mental disturbance caused by breathing gas at a reduced pressure
options.b: A disturbance caused by divers drinking too many martinis
options.d: Physical and mental disturbance caused by breathing gas at an elevated pressure

questions.explanation: Inert gas narcosis is physical and mental impairment from breathing inert gases (esp. nitrogen) at elevated partial pressures.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Why should divers carry a cutting device?

options.a: To respond to underwater entanglements hazards
options.b: To carve navigational markers into underwater rocks
options.c: To signal other divers through metal clanging
options.d: To hunt underwater creatures more effectively

questions.explanation: A cutting device is essential for freeing yourself or your buddy from line/net entanglements.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What factors can increase the risk of immersion pulmonary edema?

options.b: Diving in cold water and inspiring against a negative pressure

questions.explanation: IPE risk factors include cardiac history, cold water with negative inspiratory pressure, and exertion/stress - all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following statements about nitrox dive computers is true?

options.a: Most standard dive computers are nitrox-capable
options.b: Nitrox computers can be used for air dives
options.c: Nitrox computers calculate data based on increased oxygen levels

questions.explanation: Nitrox computers can also be used on air (set FO2 = 21%); not all dive computers are nitrox-capable.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which if the following statements about argon is true?

options.a: It is half as narcotic as nitrogen and used as a diluent gas
options.b: It has a low density and half the insulating capacity of air
options.c: It is more narcotic than nitrogen and not used as a breathing gas
options.d: It is expensive and not used as a dry suit inflation gas

questions.explanation: Argon has roughly half the narcotic potency of nitrogen; it is sometimes used as a diluent or drysuit-inflation gas.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Hypercapnia can be prevented:

options.a: By breathing in a controlled manner

questions.explanation: Controlled breathing, low workload, and well-maintained gear all help prevent CO2 buildup (hypercapnia).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are microbubbles?

options.a: Small gas bubbles that a diver breathes out through their regulator
options.b: Tiny inert gas bubbles that form in a diver's bloodstream and tissues
options.c: Tiny inert gas bubbles that dissolve in a diver's bloodstream and tissues
options.d: Small gas bubbles that form only in well-perfused tissues

questions.explanation: Microbubbles are tiny inert-gas bubbles that form in the bloodstream and tissues during/after ascent.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: At what partial pressure of oxygen can hypoxia occur?
questions.explanation: Hypoxia can occur at pO2 below approximately 0.16 bar.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Understanding the period of a wave helps divers:

options.b: Time their movements and navigate safely

questions.explanation: Wave period helps divers time entries, exits, and movements to navigate safely through surf.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following statements about carbon monoxide is true?

options.a: It is harmless and can be breathed by divers
options.c: It is not produced by the burning of fuel and oil
options.d: It is easily seen, smelled, and tasted

questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide is highly toxic, colorless, odorless, and tasteless - it binds hemoglobin far more strongly than oxygen.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How can divers minimize negative interactions with marine life?

options.a: Move cautiously, avoid sudden movements, maintain buoyancy, and avoid covered areas
options.b: Observe, but do not touch or bait animals
options.d: Wear appropriate exposure suits and follow local regulations

questions.explanation: Cautious movement, observe-don't-touch, proper exposure protection and following local rules - all minimize negative interactions.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following statements about nitrogen is true?

options.a: It is not metabolized by the body but can diffuse in and out of body tissues
options.b: Dissolved nitrogen can come out of solution and increase the risk of decompression sickness
options.c: It can cause narcosis at high partial pressures

questions.explanation: Nitrogen is inert (not metabolized) but diffuses in/out of tissues, can come out of solution causing DCS, and is narcotic at high pp - all true.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is a reverse block?

options.a: A squeeze in reverse that happens when compressing gas becomes trapped on descent
options.b: A blockage in the middle ear
options.c: A squeeze in reverse that happens when expanding gas becomes trapped on ascent
options.d: A arterial blockage caused by gas bubbles

questions.explanation: Reverse block: expanding gas trapped in an air space (sinus, ear) on ascent unable to vent, causing pain/barotrauma.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How does a gas-integrated dive computer help divers manage their gas?

options.a: By automatically adjusting the diver's breathing rate
options.c: By storing historical data about the diver's gas consumption
options.d: By monitoring and displaying the remaining gas supply in real- time

questions.explanation: Gas-integrated computers monitor cylinder pressure and display remaining gas in real time (and often calculate gas time).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Where does the greatest expansion or compression per meter occur?
questions.explanation: Boyle's law: greatest relative volume change occurs near the surface (0-10 m), where pressure doubles.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How might divers be at risk of hyperthermia?

options.b: They overheat by wearing thermal insulation in warm environments

questions.explanation: Wearing thermal insulation in warm conditions, overexertion, and inadequate hydration all raise hyperthermia risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Factors that might affect a diver's breathing gas consumption include:

options.d: Mental and physical exertion, experience, and confidence

questions.explanation: Depth/temperature/environment, personal physiology, and mental/physical state all affect gas consumption - all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the immediate care in a suspected decompression illness emergency?

options.d: Monitor vital signs and be ready to perform CPR

questions.explanation: Immediate DCI care: seek medical help, give 100% O2, monitor vitals and be ready for CPR - all correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: A dive computer algorithm considers _____ to provide conservative estimates.

options.b: Type of marine life present, and duration of dive
options.d: Dive profile, depth, time, surface interval, and gas mixture

questions.explanation: Algorithms use dive profile, depth, time, surface interval, and gas mixture to compute conservative decompression estimates.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How does hyperventilation possibly cause hypoxia?

options.a: It massively increases the amount of oxygen a diver inhales
options.b: It causes an imbalance between oxygen intake and carbon dioxide levels
options.d: It decreases blood carbon monoxide levels

questions.explanation: Hyperventilation lowers CO2, removing the breathing trigger; the diver may then black out from hypoxia before feeling air hunger.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which features distinguish a reel from a spool?

options.a: A spool is preferred for longer deployments
options.b: A reel has a handle, a brake or locking mechanism, and a winding knob
options.d: A reel is always smaller and lighter

questions.explanation: A reel has a handle, a brake/locking mechanism, and a winding knob - distinguishing it from a simple spool.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What areas of the body are typically affected by Type II decompression sickness?
questions.explanation: Type II (serious) DCS can affect the CNS, inner ear, and cardiorespiratory system - all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: A diver experiencing pain in their ears or sinuses while descending should:

options.a: Ascend until the pressure is relieved and then attempt equalization
options.b: Return to the surface and re-initiate the descent
options.d: Stop and wait until the discomfort subsides

questions.explanation: If pain develops on descent, ascend slightly until pressure is relieved, then equalize before continuing.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the partial pressure of a gas?

options.a: The sum of all the pressures of gases in a mixture
options.b: The individual pressure of a gas in a mixture of gases
options.c: The total pressure of the gas minus the gas that a diver breathes
options.d: The pressure of argon in a mixture

questions.explanation: Partial pressure = the individual pressure exerted by one gas component in a mixture (Dalton's Law).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which gas law says that pressure and volume are inversely related?
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: at constant temperature, pressure and volume of a gas are inversely proportional (P1V1 = P2V2).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the purpose of performing a safety stop during ascent?

options.a: To maintain an ascent rate of 9 meters per minute
options.b: To regulate off-gassing by making predetermined stop at a progressively shallower depth
options.c: To prevent the formation of bubbles as the gases separate out of solution
options.d: To develop a dissolved nitrogen tension higher than the surrounding pressure

questions.explanation: Safety stops regulate off-gassing by pausing at progressively shallower depths to allow controlled inert gas elimination.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are the three main groups of fishes?

options.a: Bony fishes, cartilaginous fishes, and orca
options.b: Cartilaginous fishes, bony fishes, and carnivorous fishes
options.d: Bony fishes, cartilaginous fishes, and jawless fishes

questions.explanation: The three main groups of fishes are bony fishes (Osteichthyes), cartilaginous fishes (Chondrichthyes), and jawless fishes (Agnatha).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How might helium be used in diving?

options.a: Specially trained divers use it in trimix and heliox gas mixtures to increase the effects of narcosis at depth
options.b: Specially trained divers use it in trimix and heliox gas mixtures to reduce the effects of narcosis at depth
options.c: Specially trained divers use it to inflate their dry suits for extra buoyancy
options.d: It is not used in diving

questions.explanation: Helium is used in trimix/heliox to reduce narcosis at depth, since it is far less narcotic than nitrogen.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the minimum gas required for a buddy pair with a surface air consumption of 18 liters per minute, if they want to dive to 33 meters with 12-liter cylinders, and have a 2 minutes of troubleshooting?
questions.explanation: Minimum gas (rock-bottom) for buddy pair at 33 m (4.3 ATA), 18 L/min SAC, with troubleshooting and ascent allowance, in 12 L cylinders works out to about 78 bar.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Plants and phytoplankton use carbon dioxide to:

options.d: Produce energy and release oxygen during photosynthesis

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: plants/phytoplankton use CO2 (and water + sunlight) to produce energy and release oxygen.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Which predisposing factors can increase the risk of decompression sickness?

options.a: A high fitness level, being under the age of 40, and exposure to warm water
options.b: Being well-rested, having a healthy diet, and being an experienced diver
options.c: A low fitness level, being over the age of 50, and exposure to cold water
options.d: Using loose-fitting equipment, being dehydrated, and diving in clear water

questions.explanation: Low fitness, older age, and cold-water exposure all increase DCS risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Why is it important to maintain proper stop depth control during a dive?

options.a: To increase the time required to reduce nitrogen in the tissues
options.b: To prevent oxygen toxicity during ascent
options.c: To allow the tissues to efficiently absorb nitrogen
options.d: To maximize the pressure gradient available for off-gassing and reduce the risk of decompression sickness

questions.explanation: Proper stop depth maximizes the off-gassing pressure gradient while keeping bubble growth controlled, reducing DCS risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the primary function of seagrass beds in marine ecosystems?

options.a: They provide food and habitat for various marine species
options.b: They generate waves and strong currents
options.c: They prevent all sediment from settling on the seafloor
options.d: They stop seahorses being consumed by predators

questions.explanation: Seagrass beds are primary habitat and food source for many marine species; they also stabilize sediment.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: A submersible pressure gauge is connected to the:
questions.explanation: An SPG measures cylinder pressure and connects to the regulator's high-pressure (HP) port.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is an arterial gas embolism?

options.b: The blockage of a vein by bubbles
options.c: Blue lips caused by low oxygen
options.d: The blockage of an arterial blood vessel by bubbles

questions.explanation: Arterial gas embolism (AGE) = blockage of an artery by gas bubbles, often from pulmonary barotrauma.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: The M-value is the _____ value of nitrogen.
questions.explanation: M-value = maximum tolerated tissue supersaturation (per tissue compartment) before symptoms become likely.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What are trim weights?

options.b: Adjustable weights that help the diver achieve proper trim
options.c: Weights used only at the surface to assist with flotation

questions.explanation: Trim weights are adjustable weights placed strategically (e.g., on the BC/back) to help achieve proper horizontal body trim.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Why must o-rings be properly maintained and replaced if necessary?

options.a: They help the equipment move through the water
options.b: They provide additional buoyancy to the equipment
options.c: They regulate the speed breathing gas
options.d: They prevent water from entering equipment and keep the internal parts dry

questions.explanation: O-rings seal equipment against water ingress, keeping internal parts dry; failure causes leaks and damage.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: How can waves affect diving conditions?

options.a: Larges waves in the surf zone can be hazardous
options.b: Choppy seas and rough conditions can make exits and entries difficult
options.d: Wave action can reduce underwater visibility

questions.explanation: Surf hazards, difficult entries/exits, and reduced visibility - all are ways waves affect diving.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: What is mass?

options.a: The force exerted on an object by gravity
options.b: The thermal energy of an amount of matter
options.c: The amount of matter in a given substance or object
options.d: The molecular movement in an object

questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in a given object/substance; weight is the gravitational force on that mass.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form B

questions.question: Before attempting to lift an unconscious diver into a boat or onto shore, you should:

options.b: Remove and ditch the victim's equipment

questions.explanation: Removing and ditching the victim's equipment makes the diver lighter and easier to handle, allowing safe and efficient extraction onto a boat or shore before further care can be administered.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Decisions about the best method of transportation should be based on the distance to the facility and:

options.c: The type of oxygen regulator used for first aid

questions.explanation: Both distance to the medical facility and severity of the injury drive the choice of transportation (e.g., ground vs. helicopter) so the victim reaches definitive care as quickly and safely as needed.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: If the victim is not breathing but has a pulse, your decision on your level of care will be based on:

options.c: Your distance from the next group of divers
options.d: Your distance from the shore or boat

questions.explanation: When the victim has a pulse but is not breathing, distance to shore or boat determines whether you give in-water rescue breaths (if far) or tow rapidly to a stable platform for proper rescue breathing.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: If you are separated from you dive buddy, you should:

options.b: Surface after making two 360 degree visual sweeps (1 Minute)
options.d: Start searching until your breathing gas gets low, then surface

questions.explanation: SSI's lost-buddy procedure is to look for no more than one minute (two 360-degree sweeps), then surface to reunite. This avoids escalating the situation by both divers searching at depth indefinitely.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Which level of stress is considered to be healthy?

options.a: A consistent high level of Stress.
options.b: A consistent low level of Stress, because it keeps us alert and warns us of danger.
options.c: A consistent medium level of Stress outside the water.

questions.explanation: A consistent low level of stress is healthy because it keeps us alert and aware of danger. Higher levels overwhelm coping ability, while no stress can lead to complacency.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: When you begin to tire, lack the ability to make decisions, or lend support, it is time to:
questions.explanation: When the rescuer becomes exhausted or unable to make sound decisions, calling for help prevents the rescuer from becoming a second victim and brings additional capable resources to the scene.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Physical causes of stress include:

options.a: Lack of fitness, fatigue, constriction of movement and hypothermia
options.b: Headache, fitness and a well maintained Total Diving System
options.c: Poor judgment, buddy system failure and warm water
options.d: Equipment loss, equipment failure and hyperthermia

questions.explanation: Physical causes of stress are tangible bodily factors: poor fitness, fatigue, restricted movement, and hypothermia. They directly tax the body's resources and reduce the diver's capacity to cope.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: In a buddy assist situation, which person do you have to take care first:
questions.explanation: Always take care of yourself first. A rescuer who becomes a second victim helps no one, so personal safety, breathing gas, and buoyancy are managed before assisting the buddy.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Even when no-decompression limits are strictly adhered to, the following factors can cause some divers to be more susceptible to decompression sickness:
questions.explanation: Fatigue, body weight, and dehydration all increase susceptibility to DCS even within no-deco limits. They affect off-gassing efficiency, so all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Factors which can impair movement and complicate the rescue process include:
questions.explanation: Entanglement (e.g., line, kelp) and entrapment (e.g., overhead, wreck) physically restrict a diver's movement and require special skills and tools to resolve, complicating any rescue.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: For mild and serious symptoms of DCS and Overexpansion injuries the appropriate first aid is:
questions.explanation: First aid for DCS and lung overexpansion injuries combines administering 100% oxygen, activating EMS, and monitoring vital signs — so all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: One of the best arguments in favor of always using the buddy system is:

options.a: In a stress situation there will almost always be at least two Dive Professionals which maintain control and are able to lend aid.
options.b: In a stress situation there will almost always be at least one diver who maintains control and is able to lend aid
options.c: You will always have someone which can help you setting up your Total Diving System.
options.d: You always can share your diving experiences after the dive.

questions.explanation: The core safety argument for the buddy system is that under stress at least one of the two divers is likely to remain calm and capable of helping the other.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Each diver will have slight variances in their dive plan that will affect their diving profile, especially during an entire week of diving. This is why:

options.a: Each diver must dive with their own computer
options.b: You have to stay very close together if you use one computer with you buddy
options.c: Each diver should drink more or less water
options.d: Each diver should use Nitrox 40 at the end of the week

questions.explanation: Personal profile variances over multiple days mean each diver must wear their own computer so the device tracks that individual's actual exposure and residual nitrogen accurately.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of lung overexpansion injuries include:
questions.explanation: Breathing difficulties and chest pain are classic signs of lung overexpansion injuries (e.g., pneumothorax, mediastinal emphysema). Joint pain is more typical of DCS.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: The following are causes of psychological stress:

options.a: Lack of fitness, fatigue, constriction of movement and hypothermia
options.b: Equipment loss, equipment failure and hyperthermia
options.c: Headache, fitness and a well maintained Total Diving System
options.d: Lack of mental preparedness, mental control, and mental coping

questions.explanation: Psychological stress comes from the mind: lack of mental preparedness, control, and coping skills. The other lists describe physical/environmental factors.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Each and every safe dive starts with?

options.c: Entering the water with an giant stride.
options.d: Entering the water with a back-roll

questions.explanation: Pre-dive planning establishes objectives, limits, gas, conditions, and emergency procedures. Without it, every other step is built on assumptions instead of an agreed plan.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Common skills for self-aid are:

options.b: Using alternate fin kicks and relieving leg cramps
options.d: Switching to alternate air sources and regulator retrieval

questions.explanation: All listed skills — buoyancy control, emergency ascents, alternate fin kicks, cramp relief, alternate air, regulator retrieval — are core self-aid skills, so all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: The two most common dive maladies are:
questions.explanation: The two most common pressure-related dive maladies are lung overexpansion injuries and decompression sickness. Both arise from the gas-pressure dynamics of scuba diving.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Preparation in which area may be the life-saving difference in a panic or rescue situation?
questions.explanation: Accident management training teaches you how to coordinate the entire response — assessment, EMS activation, first aid, and evacuation — and is often the life-saving differentiator in a real emergency.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: The main reason people run out of breathing gas is:

options.c: Failure to make visual inspection every year.
options.d: Failure to monitor the pressure gauge or air integrated dive computer.
